topic: Describe a life event which you feel has prepared you to be successful in college.
content:
If I could recommend one place to visit in Taiwan, it would be the mangrove forest at Taijiang National Park. It is where I have gotten closest to nature. Boating through its green tunnels as a kid was a therapeutic experience. I felt like I was conversing directly with nature. However, one year, outside noise interrupted this dialogue. Dozens of dead fish littered the surface of the water, turning the once scenic tunnels into sepulchers. The culprit: fertilizer leakage.

This marred childhood memory was one of my first brushes with the often-fraught relationship between humanity and nature. It spurred a passion for keeping our planet safe and flourishing. I attended beach cleanings and advocated for the need of strong environmental measures. I also developed a strong academic interest for the interaction between nature and its human residents. This led me to compete in the Geography Olympiad and learn more about the impact of climate change and ways to alleviate it.

We inhabit a beautiful planet, and we are treating it poorly. However, we will be the ones who suffer for this mistreatment in the end. Climate change and environmental collapse will devastate us, but Earth will carry on. I want to keep our planet safe to preserve its nature beauty and to preserve ourselves. Once I am in college, I will keep exploring and experiencing the beauty of our planet and learning about how to keep that beauty alive. My conversation with nature will continue deep into the night.

Tell us about the person who has most impacted your life and why.*

Milutin Milanković not only shed light on the theorem of earth’s climatic pattern in millions of years of scale using Milankovitch Cycles, but he also brightened up my interest, earth science. This renowned Serbian geophysicist was the very first person to theorize climate change regarding the periods of how interaction between Earth and the Sun’s movement fluctuates. Of course, he didn’t just make inferences, but proved it with detailed evidence. 

Milanković found that the concentration of greenhouse gases is exceptionally high in certain layers of ice caps in Antarctica. With this in mind, he proceeded to calculate precise Earth's movement within the solar system and finally concluded the Milankovitch Cycles. Milanković is undoubtedly a well-famed scientist, but what really captivates me is how meticulous and logical he was when approaching science. While staying rational, at the instance, he was a man of bold hypotheses. His unconstrained and comprehensive thinking style definitely took astronomical and geological science to new heights.

	As one of the billion citizens under the influence of climate change, I want to follow the route of Milutin Milanković. I envision myself as a meticulous earth scientist driven by curiosity to explore the world. I aim not solely at acquiring an abundance of knowledge, but to execute them in feasible solutions to combat climate change. I look forward to inspiring hope, empowering communities, and safeguarding the future of our planet for the newborn generations.

topic: Briefly elaborate on one of your extracurricular activities, a job you hold, or responsibilities you have for your family.
content: At Ross, our motto was to “think deeply about simple things.” When we finally proved prime factorization, I realized the theorems we took for granted actually involved deeper truths like Bezout’s identity and Euclid’s Lemma. Now, I probe “simple” moments in life, knowing even the obvious can hide something profound.

topic: Emory University has a strong commitment to building community. Tell us about a community you have been part of where your participation helped to change or shape the community for the better.
content:
When I was born, our house was surrounded by farmlands. As I grew up, our community was redeveloped into a technology district, and I enrolled in the newly built international school on a scholarship. Unlike my friends’ parents, who all had advanced degrees, my parents never graduated from high school or learned English. However, my school was incredibly supportive; peers volunteered to practice English with me, and teachers provided hours of extra help.

When I reached high school, I desired to give back by making knowledge more accessible. I co-founded the Science Magazine, helping aspiring writers make contemporary discoveries understandable to middle schoolers. I also founded Investing Lab and taught eager students how good trades stem from a clear sight of the market and its interplay with the overarching economy. Disadvantaged students need resources and confidence to succeed, and I will always try my best to pass them on.

topic: Tell us why you would like to attend the University of Wisconsin–Madison. In addition, please include why you are interested in studying the major(s) you have selected. If you selected undecided please describe your areas of possible academic interest.
content:
I love rocks. My fascination started when I pushed a piece of pumice into a lake and saw it float. This contradicted everything I thought I knew. My curiosity was piqued and has only grown since. I have found that each rock has its own story. Together, they form an index of the history of our planet. It is a history defined by cycles, from the life cycles of the tiniest bugs to the movement of the plates and the shifting of the magnetic field. I am fascinated by my rock collection, these cycles, and everything else that makes up this planet of ours; I want to study and understand it all!

However, I do not only want to understand. I want to make our planet a better place to live by fighting climate change and preserving our environment. Tackling such daunting, complex issues will require expertise in a variety of intersecting fields. For that reason, I seek a program with an interdisciplinary focus and plenty of opportunity to branch out.

Moreover, I want to get my hands dirty. During the finals of the Geography Olympiad, I conducted field research for the first time. I trekked through various trails, local residences, and tourist spots in Xindian and examined how coal mining culture and fluvial erosion influenced local land use patterns. I loved studying the geology and erosion patterns up close. They revealed how natural forces can shape landscapes over time. Similarly, my treks along abandoned coal and copper mines revealed the impact of human activity on its environment. These experiences showed me the value of combining theoretical study with practical experience.

These fascinations, ambitions, and experiences have guided me to the Geography major at UW Madison. I am excited to further my study of our planet with courses like ‘Physical Systems of the Environment’, ‘Glacial and Pleistocene Geology’, and ‘Human Transformations of Earth Surface Processes’. Meanwhile, courses like ‘Global Warming: Science and Impacts’ and ‘Environmental Conservation’ perfectly attend to my desire to help preserve our planet. However, what really speaks to me is the diversity of approaches even within the program itself. I am excited to dip my toes into different fields like sociology, math, and critical theory with courses such as ‘Revolutions and Social Change’, ´Researching the City: Qualitative Strategies´, and ´Critical Indigenous Ecological Knowledges´. This is exactly the kind of academic breadth that I am looking for.

However, my interest in UW Madison goes well beyond its diverse course list. I am deeply intrigued by the research conducted by the faculty professors. I am especially interested in Professor Mason´s research into geomorphology and paleoenvironments. His studies on eolian and hillslope geomorphology in China has given me fascinating new insights into wind erosion and the way it shapes environments. I would love to participate in his study of paleoenvironments to trace wind patterns from millennia ago. Such research could improve our understanding of the correlations and differences in climate patterns during catastrophic climate conditions. This in turn could help us better grasp the possible effects of climate change today.

Finally, my interest in UW Madison is not limited solely to academics. As a passionate mountaineer, I am particularly interested in the student chapter of The Wildlife Society for its emphasis on wilderness and care for wildlife. The club´s enthusiasm for the outdoors and its regular hosting of professional speakers who share advice on future careers makes me excited to join their ranks. I already look forward to making new international friends through a shared love for outdoor activities and the environment.

UW Madison has everything I am looking for in my college career. Between the diverse courses and inspiring professors of its Geography major and its exciting extracurricular opportunities, I cannot think of a better place to continue my studies. I cannot wait to enrich my understanding of our planet and meet my fellow rock-loving Badgers!

topic: Tell us why you would like to attend the University of Wisconsin–Madison. In addition, please include why you are interested in studying the major(s) you have selected. If you selected undecided, please describe your areas of possible academic interest.
content: Through the Aspiring Scholars Directed Research Program (ASDRP), I have had the opportunity to explore biosensing and the filtration and detection of targeted molecules, which is beneficial for developing technologies for the early detection of any diseases in general and also for innovating new treatments or personalized medicine. This made me realize that my research can improve social welfare, driving me to study biology at the University of Wisconsin-Madison (UWM). To prepare for advanced research, I delved into how each enzyme functions and the action potential process; experimenting in the electrophoresis lab, I found that DNA is not only special because of the different nucleotide sequences but also the variance in length. Building on my AP Biology foundation, I developed a diffusion model in which we can put a blockade in the middle and control the diffusion of fluorescein molecules as an ASDRP group leader under the guidance of a professor. At UWM, I intend to refine my research and interpersonal skills through the Biocore program to gain more experience via lectures and laboratory lectures. Outreach opportunities like the Center for Leadership and Involvement will also allow me to practice my leadership skills, including communication. Additionally, I am interested in The Cellular and Molecular Biology of Stress Summer Research Program, a full-time biology research program where I can acquire knowledge about data analysis and continue independent research. With a specific research emphasis on the lipid bilayer, this program focuses on how cells detect and respond to stress, making it a great opportunity to continue and further my research on biosensing. As a biology tutor who guides underclassmen through their first or second year of high school, I would give my students a lecture about the materials, like the four types of macromolecules. Then, I would help them with their homework and teach them new concepts as we engage in games like Kahoot! when reviewing the questions. When my students were confused about the difference between saturated and unsaturated fat, I drew the structures of both kinds of fat with online or school complementary readings to enhance their understanding. During the process, I gained critical insights into maintaining a supportive classroom environment while transforming abstract textbook materials into comprehensible concepts. At UWM, I aim to participate in the Precollege Enrichment Opportunity Program for Learning Excellence to continue volunteering to mentor students in subjects like biology, which I am passionate about and relatively familiar with, and offer other students in need approaches to prepare for college courses and challenges. My experience as a tutor has strengthened my communication and problem-solving skills, which are essential for being a successful biologist. As a group project leader in my research program, I have been an independent and thoughtful researcher who developed strategies for myself to achieve the end goal of biosensing. At UWM, I picture myself helping others and being able to learn more essential skills and knowledge of biology, the subject I am passionate about.

topic: Our families and communities often define us and our individual worlds. Community might refer to your cultural group, extended family, religious group, neighborhood or school, sports team or club, co-workers, etc. Describe the world you come from and how you, as a product of it, might add to the diversity of the University of Washington.
content:
As a member of the orchestra, my sense of community extends beyond conventional boundaries. The harmony of collaboration and emotional support within the orchestra has not only shaped my musical journey but also significantly influenced my perspective. It has instilled in me a deep appreciation for sharing emotions and caring for others. This isn't just an incidental outcome; rather, it's a habit cultivated by our conductor. Under his guidance, we leave behind the traditional confines of individual practice, forming blocks of three peers who practice together. This communal approach to rehearsals transforms the experience, making it less stressful and more intense in its collaborative energy.

Practicing with peers creates an environment where we play an unignorable role in the improvement process. Our communal effort enables us to notice mistakes and make corrections collectively, fostering an atmosphere where I feel mutual support and get and provide practical feedback. It was within these small groups that I learned new techniques, like the art of bow jumping, and offered advice to my peers on playing more emotionally with techniques such as vibrato.

I believe that the emphasis on practicing together, sharing ideas, and offering constructive feedback in the orchestra has even helped us to talk more outside of orchestra This, in turn, prevents and reduces conflicts and creates camaraderie. More than that, we are friends. Where we once were fighting over chair positions, now we are embracing our shared admiration of pieces by Webber and Shostakovich.

As a participant in the orchestra community, I bring to the University of Washington a unique perspective on collaboration and emotional expression, and the strength those bring to a collective effort. My experiences have taught me that unity in diversity is not just a slogan but a living reality, one that I am eager to contribute to.

topic: Our families and communities often define us and our individual worlds. Community might refer to your cultural group, extended family, religious group, neighborhood or school, sports team or club, co-workers, etc. Describe the world you come from and how you, as a product of it, might add to the diversity of the University of Washington.
content:
​I’m proud to be senior patrol leader of Troop 99 in the Far East Council of the Boy Scouts of America (BSA). BSA is a true global community: at the recent jamboree in Nepal, scouts came from over 50 countries; Christians, Hindus, and Aetheists like me; those okay with curry for 6 days straight (Indian and local Nepalese scouts) and those not (American scouts). 

 

​​As scouts, we live by the Scout Law: follow the rules, help other people, stay strong. The scout motto tells us to be prepared and the slogan tells us to do good daily. All the scouts were living out these ideals during the Nepal campout, checking for leeches on each other’s backs or preparing rain gear at the first trace of thunderstorm clouds. Everything worked well because we all knew what to do: provide help to others without hesitation while leaving no trace on the environment. Despite our many different backgrounds, it was amazing to see the same spirit running through us all. 

 

In my troop, my job is to cultivate that spirit by ensuring that everything is running properly and keeping an eye on every scout so that no one falls behind or feels isolated. Even if BSA is huge, spreading all over the world, every troop is like a tiny gear: crucial to the workings of the bigger machine. I do my best to keep my troop well-oiled and connected to the global community by upholding our shared principles: respecting and supporting one another all the time.

 

​My experiences in BSA have shaped me into a leader who understands the strength of diversity. I believe my perspective and commitment to fostering inclusivity will contribute to the diversity of the University of Washington.

topic: Describe how you plan to pursue your academic interests at USC. Please feel free to address your first- and second-choice major selections.
content:
I believe that the most important trend in business, both short- and long-term, is the integration of AI into everything. How can businesses use AI to pursue efficiency and profitability without sacrificing customer experience? What kind of role can AIs play in business strategy? And how can we minimize the impact on sustainability given the requirements of large-scale computation? 

To answer these questions, I plan to pursue USC’s unique AI for Business program (BUAI). The integrative classes on digital transformation and technology strategy, such as BUAD 112 and DSO 429, provide contact and experience with industry professionals at the forefront of this constantly evolving sector. I am also drawn to the major’s emphasis on Data Science, which is my second choice major. As Viterbi focuses heavily on engineering, I appreciate that business classes and peer discussions there are infused with a strong technological perspective. 

Outside of class, I would take every opportunity to get involved with the Experimental Learning Center, as my experiences in The Stock Market Game have made me realize I thrive in hands-on learning environments. Given the unique ethical challenges of AI in business, I also intend to get involved with research at the Neely Center, ideally starting as a research assistant in the AI Index longitudinal tracking survey. Finally, I would like to do internships via ELO and learn how AI is applied in realistic business contexts, anticipating and shaping the “far-reaching societal implications” of artificial intelligence.

topic: How did you spend your last two summers?
content:
2025: Researched Amis, Atayal, and Paiwan translations; studied abstract algebra and watched July 4th fireworks at Ross Mathematics Camp.

2024: Built and patented my AI Smart-Planting Box, taught English at local elementary, karaoke-ed on a family California road trip (visited Stanford!)

Both: Delved into Neural Networks & NLP; grinded USACO problems.

topic: What academic areas are you interested in exploring at Emory University and why?
content:
Money interests me because I want to be a doctor. Not a doctor of the human body, but of organizations. A nugget of wisdom from my favorite movie, The Big Short, imparts that the flow of money reflects the economic health of firms or even nations. Many of the “patients” do not know that they are ill, and those who do often find themselves lacking professional guidance. This is why I am committed to filling the gaps using the hard truths that finance reveals.

However, finance does not always tell the full story, just as there is no universal cure for all diseases. Therefore, I aspire to put more types of medicine in my kit. An important one is Data Science, as it is essential to interpreting and modeling macroeconomic trends. Another is Psychology, because cognitive and social factors influence financial decisions on individual and societal levels.

At Emory, I can not only explore career potential as a “doctor” with the resources of a research university, but also sustain my commitment to the “Hippocratic Oath” with Emory’s liberal arts education and its emphasis on the purpose as well as the outcome of work.

topic: Tell us why you would like to attend the University of Wisconsin-Madison. In addition, please include why you are interested in studying the major(s) you have selected. If you selected undecided please describe your areas of possible academic interest.
content:
Jewelry tools clinked in my father’s tiny workshop, each chime a reminder that vision and persistence can transform raw materials into something priceless. Before I knew what an entrepreneur was, I watched my father shape rough metals into delicate jewelry. My job was often small—polishing pendants or organizing beads—but I saw how each meticulous step mattered, how careful effort could turn an ordinary fragment into a meaningful piece. I learned early on that success often begins with small, deliberate actions, repeated day after day until something extraordinary emerges.

That lesson proved invaluable when I joined my high school’s iGEM team to develop a dengue prevention project. At first, we faced a flurry of challenges: complicated lab protocols, unexpected experimental failures, and the daunting question of how to fund our research. It felt like standing in my father’s workshop again—staring at raw materials, unsure of the outcome. But I remembered how my father would patiently file away imperfections, adapting his design as he went. Inspired by his approach, I focused on refining our team’s plan. We conducted extensive research on mosquito-borne illnesses, collaborated with faculty mentors, and contacted local healthcare agencies for insights on community needs. Eventually, we developed a re-engineered microbe aimed at reducing mosquito populations. Though small, this innovation showed me how an idea—nurtured by patience, resilience, and teamwork—can make a tangible difference in the real world.

As I look ahead to college, I see UW–Madison as an environment that values creativity and action—exactly where I can continue bridging science and business to tackle pressing global issues. The Wisconsin School of Business stands out to me for its curriculum that weaves strategic thinking with practical application. Courses such as Business Strategy and Entrepreneurial Management in Practice will equip me with the tools to translate bold ideas into concrete solutions. Equally important, programs like the Weikel Leadership Program and the Center for Professional and Executive Development will allow me to sharpen my ability to guide teams with diverse skill sets.

I look forward to learning strategies for fostering collaboration, ensuring every voice is heard, and creating a cohesive vision—skills I relied on during iGEM.

Beyond the classroom, UW–Madison’s entrepreneurial ecosystem excites me. The Weinert Center for Entrepreneurship and the StartUp Learning Community are hubs where I can transform biotech concepts into ventures to address public health or environmental challenges. I see myself collaborating with students from the College of Agricultural and Life Sciences or partnering with engineering majors to refine prototypes. Together, we could participate in campus pitch competitions or statewide innovation challenges, sharpening our ideas through teamwork and diverse perspectives.

Yet what excites me most is the broader culture at UW–Madison—a place known for intellectual curiosity, scenic surroundings, and a genuine sense of community. I imagine myself unwinding by Lake Mendota after a day in the lab or brainstorming sessions, inspired by the natural beauty and the lively campus atmosphere. I’m eager to join organizations like Business Badgers and student clubs that blend social entrepreneurship, science outreach, and community service. Whether volunteering locally or presenting research at undergraduate symposiums, I want to immerse myself in a campus that celebrates bold thinking and collective growth.

Ultimately, UW–Madison represents more than just a place to learn—it’s a place to grow. Just as my father’s workshop shaped my values of perseverance and creativity, I believe the university will refine my skills and ambitions. I look forward to contributing my perspective—part science enthusiast, part entrepreneur—to a community that thrives on innovation. With UW–Madison’s support, I’m confident I can transform ideas into reality, forging a path that honors the lessons I learned amid the gentle clink of jewelry tools and the spark of scientific discovery.

topic: What academic areas are you interested in exploring at Emory University and why?
content:
My interest in Biology started to grow when my brother shared his gross but fascinating thoughts about his bowel movements. He imagined it as a massive army collectively pushing a giant monster out of the gate. From then on, this idea stuck in my head, and I began to pay attention to every detail I felt in my body, creating scenarios to explain them. 

My curiosity deepened the first time I uncovered the mystery under our skin while conducting a perfusion on rodents in the lab. The tiny heart palpitates, and the moment I felt its rhythm, I realized that biology is like our closest stranger; it is a part of us, yet we don’t know everything about it. 

Although I’m drawn to becoming a front-line provider, conducting research remains one of my greatest passions. At Emory, the dual degree program especially excites me, as it allows me to develop in-depth biological knowledge while also gaining hands-on clinical experience through the School of Nursing. Additionally, I look forward to exploring research opportunities, particularly the Shepherd Lab, which focuses on the development of the enteric nervous system (ENS), as well as research collaboration in the School of Nursing at Emory.

topic: What is the most significant challenge that society faces today?
content: Lingua Franca: Common language for communication used by communities with different native tongue. While it bridges cultures, it can also cause minority languages to fade as people adopt prevailing languages for education or job opportunities. Thus, the real challenge is not to resist change, but to balance this increased connectedness with the protection of unique voices.

topic: Our families and communities often define us and our individual worlds. Community might refer to your cultural group, extended family, religious group, neighborhood or school, sports team or club, co-workers, etc. Describe the world you come from and how you, as a product of it, might add to the diversity of the University of Washington.
content:
Growing up, I would often labor away my summers in my second home—the teacher’s piano room—practicing for countless hours each day. From fiddling about with the piano at age five to participating in numerous regional competitions in high school, I have always had an affinity for the instrument. Initially striving for accuracy and eventually adding my own flair, I have cohabited each phase of my upbringing with a constant companion – music.

In high school, I decided to challenge myself in a different way. Instead of just performing, I volunteered to co-host my music school’s annual concert. This new position helped me realize the importance of creating a supportive and enriching environment for fledgling musicians to grow together. Realizing that students were often practicing alone in isolated time slots, I began to assign new students together with veteran musicians, coordinating performance schedules and organizing activities to foster a stronger sense of camaraderie. In doing so, we created a nurturing environment where students would feel comfortable seeking guidance from more experienced musicians, leading many to step outside their comfort zones, opting for more challenging pieces. The concert was ultimately a success, but the true achievement was seeing the students, who not long ago were total strangers, celebrating together with their new family. 

At UW, I am excited to extend my music inclusivity efforts to the Husky community. I am eager to join Music for Charity at the University of Washington, facilitating performances and connecting with peers to collaborate on charity work. Furthermore, I hope to join FIUTS, organizing events to continue building inclusive environments with my fellow international students. By utilizing my experiences in music and event organization, I hope to spark genuine connections, empowering myself and others to carry our talents beyond the stage and into the broader community.

topic: Share a time when you were most proud of yourself either as a role model or when you displayed your leadership. What specific skills did you contribute to the experience? How did others rely on you for guidance? What did you learn about yourself during this time?
content: I'm proud to have become a leader in the Stack Overflow community. Having received valuable guidance on Stack Overflow throughout my own programming journey, during the past few summers, I spent an hour daily answering questions regarding the web-development framework Django. My answers eventually received 138K views, putting me in the top 0.3% upvoted for the entire platform in 2020. Original posters would often follow up, with conversations lasting from 15 minutes to two days. From this experience, I recognized my strong motivation to help new programmers as others helped me. I found my talent for simplifying complicated solutions: instead of throwing out abstract concepts and jargon, I incorporated examples, detailed explanations, and external references for self-directed learners.

topic: Fordham, as a Jesuit university, recognizes the dignity, uniqueness and potential of each person. A Fordham education is student-centered and rooted in close collaboration among students, faculty, and staff. Describe how you would contribute to our campus community as an actively engaged learner and leader. Specifically draw on your personal story, identity, experiences, strengths, and perspectives.
content:
I’m somewhat afraid my community at Fordham will know me as “that guy who whistles.” I love whistling wherever I go; it’s like carrying an invisible instrument around with me. What I cherish most is that there are no prerequisites, no work experience or advanced music theory required. I’d love to meet other whistlers at Fordham–or at least try to join the F Sharps–but more importantly, I aspire to make my passions and knowledge as fun and accessible to others as whistling is to non-musical me.

	For example, the magazines I read as a child sparked my interest in subjects ranging from the separation of powers in the U.S. Constitution to luminescent fish. Similarly, in The Observer, I would use my experience as EIC of my school’s science magazine and contribute articles to make contemporary news and knowledge more accessible for the community at Fordham. Additionally, I hope to convince my friends to join the Coding Club. There, I imagine perpetual mutual learning as I share my experiences in computer science and learn from peers. Not only is coding exciting, but I also believe it is a necessary tool that opens doors for both career and social impact.

	Conversely, I also hope to hear the invisible instruments that my classmates carry around with them. For example, I would join the Football / Soccer Fan Club at Fordham because my lack of skill barred me from joining my high school team. Even more, I would like to participate in Deaf Education and American Sign Language (DEASL). As I have multiple family members with lifelong disabilities, I have learned to cherish the instruments I was born with and am determined to help those whose instruments are less understood.

topic: We are interested in learning more about you. Please respond to the prompt below with a response of no more than 200 words. This response should be different and distinct from the one used for your main Common Application essay. The University of San Francisco's Jesuit tradition emphasizes community engagement and education for social justice, inspiring our students to become passionate agents for others. How do you see yourself becoming a part of this mission?
content:
I envision my role at USF as a student who blends business knowledge with a strong sense of community responsibility. Working on my high school’s iGEM team taught me that real solutions require more than technical expertise—they demand empathy, understanding, and a commitment to addressing real needs. USF’s Jesuit mission to promote social justice resonates deeply with my goal to use business as a tool for positive impact.

At USF, I look forward to studying Business Administration and International Business through a socially responsible lens. The opportunity to engage in community outreach and connect with peers and faculty who value compassion and justice will strengthen my resolve to build businesses that prioritize accessibility and equity. My aim is to grow as a person and a future business leader who can create ventures that uplift communities and drive meaningful change.

topic: Our families and communities often define us and our individual worlds. Community might refer to your cultural group, extended family, religious group, neighborhood or school, sports team or club, co-workers, etc. Describe the world you come from and how you, as a product of it, might add to the diversity of the University of Washington.
content:
A sense of accomplishment bubbled in me as Jaden showed me his A+ for the biology final. I was delighted to see Jaden’s growth as he worked diligently throughout the year. In the last semester, we worked together to research different types of pollution and ways to address them, such as turning off lights to reduce light pollution. By helping him, I also learned to maximize school resources to gather detailed topic-related information easily.

To start my designed lesson plans, I would give my students a lecture about the materials to lay

the groundwork. Afterward, I would help them with their homework and teach them new concepts we find in the homework as we proceed. Lastly, I tested them on the topics with games like Kahoot! and reviewed questions they got wrong. Seeing how students gradually improved their grades after my extensive and repeated explanation, I learned that I should always try to

incorporate various teaching methods to aid in my students’ comprehension. For example, I also

developed a detailed to-do list with time intervals, indicating the specific use of each hour at night; this improved Jaden’s time management in navigating unfinished tasks.

As a teacher, I renewed my knowledge while enhancing my patience and teaching skills, including curriculum design, public speaking, and self-confidence; I was happy to see my students grow. At the University of Washington, I will continue to help and tutor others through programs like biology study groups under the Center for Learning and Undergraduate Enrichment (CLUE) tutoring or residence hall tutoring and to make the school environment a more welcoming and supportive place.

topic: What about being a student at BU most excites you, and how do you hope to contribute to our campus community? This prompt asks why you are excited about BU and how you will add to its community. You should research BU’s programs, student organizations, and opportunities that interest you and explain how these fit with your personal goals and experiences. Be sure to also mention how your unique traits or perspectives will enhance BU’s campus culture.
content:
What if human reflexes could rival those of science fiction heroes like Cyberpunk’s “Sandevistan” users? This question sparked my fascination with synthetic biology and inspired me to explore how genetic engineering can turn ambitious ideas into tangible solutions. Boston University’s Synthetic Biology Initiative and emphasis on interdisciplinary research make it the perfect place to pursue these goals.

As team leader for my iGEM project, I developed a dengue prevention strategy using Cyt2Ba toxins and geosmin. Beyond our scientific breakthroughs, this experience taught me how research connects science with community impact. Engaging with local health officials deepened my commitment to ensuring biotechnology solutions address societal needs. At BU, I am excited to continue this work through the Summer Undergraduate Research Fellowship, collaborating with peers to create meaningful, globally-minded solutions.

BU’s BUild Lab is another exciting resource, where I hope to explore entrepreneurial approaches to synthetic biology, such as accessible medical diagnostics or sustainable technologies. I’m particularly inspired by the work of Dr. Wilson Wong, whose BLADE platform embodies the kind of innovative research I aspire to undertake. His focus on designing synthetic gene circuits that adapt and respond aligns perfectly with my interest in blending biology with engineering to solve real-world problems.

Beyond academics, I’m eager to contribute to BU’s collaborative and dynamic community. Whether through the BU iGEM team or interdisciplinary projects that combine science with ethics and policy, I look forward to fostering a culture of creativity and impact. At BU, I hope to join like-minded peers and faculty in pushing the boundaries of what is possible, creating solutions that bridge innovation and community needs.

topic: Tell us why you decided to apply to the University of Wisconsin-Madison. In addition, please include why you are interested in studying the major(s) you have selected. If you selected undecided please describe your areas of possible academic interest.
content:
From my freshman year onward, the world of computers became an enticing ocean, compelling me to dive deeper and deeper. I began my exploration of computer networks by setting up a server on the video game "Minecraft." This was a moment that fully engaged me and made me lose track of time.

The moment I decided to major in computer science was when I wanted to create a server for my friends to play together. However, it turned out to be more complex than I initially thought. It involved tasks like configuring port settings and managing dynamic IP address translation. The University of Wisconsin-Madison offers a wealth of courses that align with my desire to delve deeper into understanding how the Internet interacts and connects securely, without compromising personal information. Some of these courses include COMP SCI 640 — Introduction to Computer Networks, COMP SCI 642 — Introduction to Information Security, COMP SCI/​E C E 707 — Mobile and Wireless Networking, COMP SCI 740 — Advanced Computer Networks, and COMP SCI 763 — Security and Privacy for Data Science. Among these, my favorite course is COMP SCI/​E C E 782 — Advanced Computer Security and Privacy. This course focuses on security and privacy issues in software, networks, and hardware systems, which are closely related to networking, security, and modern machine learning. As someone who has been intrigued by setting up servers and configuring network settings, I understand the importance of networking security and privacy. These courses will enable me to explore the theoretical aspects of these topics.

On the recreational side of things, I am drawn to the UW-Madison Symphony Orchestra. Music has been an integral part of my life since I was four years old. As a viola player, I serve as a compromiser, influencing the tonal colors of the pieces. The role of the viola holds a special place in my heart. This orchestra provides me with the opportunity to continue pursuing my long-standing passion alongside my major. One particular memory that vividly reinforces this truth is from an orchestra summer camp in the US in 2022: BUTI. It was there that I discovered a shared passion and an unspoken musical philosophy with many others. It felt as though we were engaging in a conversation without words, conveying our deepest emotions, thoughts, and aspirations through the universal language of music. I have found a similar environment at UW-Madison, where many contemporary improvisation players come together. I am eager to continue developing various styles and forms of musical performances with my peers. 

I believe that UW-Madison University has the qualities to enhance my journey in both engineering and music. Its incredible opportunities with Computer Science and its distinct music performance would give me access to stand at the front of the networking world accompanied by my favorite humanity activity.

topic: Why is the University of Wisconsin–Madison a strong fit for you and your college goals?
content:
UW-Madison's strong engineering program in combination with its famed sports culture are what continue to draw me to UW-Madison. In the past few months, I have become a starter on the Volleyball team and earned a ski coach certification while continuing my leadership role on the basketball team, planning and going on cycling trips with my team, and training the junior volleyball players who will take my place next year. Sports are a huge part of my life: it would be amazing to fully immerse myself with UW-Madison’s sports vibes and be the #1 fan at all of the games.

My previous engineering experience has been mostly focused on software and game development, but as I’ve been taking AP Physics and Statistics this year, I’m also interested in branching out and exploring other kinds of engineering such as Mechanical or Aerospace. UW-Madison’s many programs and the students and professors doing work across these many disciplines would be most helpful to guiding me to my ultimate engineering destination, and finding success in that field.

With its large student population and many programs, UW-Madison is where I believe I can settle on my path and be among peers who share the same passion for sports that I do. I look forward to experiencing the college experience fully, and there would be no better place to do so than UW-Madison.

topic: Describe your personal and/or career goals after graduating from Illinois and how your selected first-choice major will help you achieve them.
content: My career goal after graduation is to contribute to society with the knowledge I have gained by developing personalized treatments and ways of early diagnosis with biosensing. Research programs like MCB Honors Concentration will help me gain more knowledge in cellular biology, the lipid bilayer membrane, and cellular structures while refining my research acumen and public speaking skills. From this program, I strive to polish my academic writing skills and learn and practice collaborating with researchers to create new, meaningful progress in biology. I would also like to take Microbiology to immerse myself in the realm of molecular biology and its importance in human health, building a foundation for me to continue my research about cell membrane structure and functions under the research opportunities that UIUC offers. Overall, UIUC is a university where I would like to pursue my interest in biology.

topic: Why do you want to attend Fordham University, and how will you contribute to our academic and campus community?
content:
At Fordham, I aspire to contribute my experience to the Frank J. Petrilli Center for Research in International Finance. As my father was an avid investor, we would frequently investigate economic environments in East and Southeast Asia; this, combined with my experience as a long-term resident in Taiwan, fostered my unique expertise and perspective towards Asian financial markets. Furthermore, I hope to bring my research abilities and intense work ethic to the research center. I was my group’s main researcher during the Wharton Global Youth program, gathering all necessary information within the first few days of the week our group has. I even contacted an engineer at Google; even though I place efficiency in high regard, what matters most is that our information quality went above and beyond the minimum requirements. In the end, we received an award for the best poster, a kind of distinction I hope to replicate at this university. 

Additionally, I am eager to collaborate with fellow journalists in The Observer. I am currently the chief editor of our school’s science magazine club. At peak times, I would review two articles a day on average, making sure to return them with comment-filled margins within a week. This is not to mention that I am also the co-founder of the club; I actively recruited members, provided ideas for new articles, and often planned overnight with my peers to discuss deadlines, reader feedback, and content difficulty. Even though managing the two positions may be tiresome, seeing members improve in their penmanship and seeing readers indulging themselves in our hard work immediately washes my fatigue away. Though I do not anticipate holding a leadership position within my first year, I am excited to help those in the club with my experience and passion for journalism.

topic: Reflect on a social or community issue that deeply resonates with you. Why is it important to you, and how have you been involved in addressing or raising awareness about it?
content:
“Joseph is smart”

“Nick is rich”

“York got more A than I did”

I am living in a city, where we compare everything, whether material possessions or academic performance. From a young age, I was taught to measure success against these external markers, creating a perpetual cycle of comparison that leads to feelings of inadequacy and unworthiness. 

The issue is particularly significant to me because I’ve compared everything I learned to my sisters, relatives, and close friends. It’s almost as if this habit of comparison is a reflex, shaping our self-worth based on how we stack up against others. 

One particular example was when I struggled with not being at the top of the class. Seeing “York got more A’s than I did” became more than just a statement, I became jealous and started my mental comparison with him in every single quiz and assignment. This obsession led me to seek shortcuts to achieve higher grades, often relying on searching for answers online or even copying others’ work into my project, all driven by the single desire to “get more A than he did”. 

I won this duel.

Yet, “So what?” 

Looking back, I was lost. Apart from chasing York, I haven’t grown substantially. Constantly fulfilling requirements and others’ expectations, I felt like I was a shadow, always trailing behind others - lack of creation, direction, and motivation. I was lost. 

Comparison is detrimental to a healthy self-worth. This taught me a valuable lesson: The importance of focusing on personal growth and development rather than perpetually measuring myself against others because we are all different. I learned to always set a goal for myself, whether it is as modest as completing homework every day or as ambitious as being a millionaire. As I witness that I’ve done something better than the previous me, the compulsion to compare with others fades away. I have learned to look beyond external achievement and focus on inner growth. 

It seems to me that comparison exists as a human nature and it would not be easy to eliminate.  However, I believe a growth model is a mentality worth promoting. I have planted a seed in myself for a future thriving and spreading.

topic: Our families and communities often define us and our individual worlds. Community might refer to your cultural group, extended family, religious group, neighborhood or school, sports team or club, co-workers, etc. Describe the world you come from and how you, as a product of it, might add to the diversity of the UW.
content:
“Are you hiking solo? Nice job, young man!”

Elderly hikers in flip flops flying by on difficult trails and shouting encouragements is a uniquely Taiwanese phenomenon. Through countless hikes, I joined this community of freakishly fit seniors and learned many lessons along the way.

I did not start off my hiking career with a community mindset. I began solely focused on my personal achievements. I solo hiked a 10,000-foot mountain every two weeks and summited the ten highest peaks on the island. Only after two falls and a handful of conversations with fellow hikers did I begin to consider alternative joys of hiking. 

Once I started talking with the elderly hikers, my treks were transformed. They told me stories about Taiwan’s history that gave me a deeper understanding of the trails that had been paved by the generations before me. I learned that these paths did not simply connect point A to point B but reflected the rich history of our country. Climbing together with the seniors elevated my appreciation for history and nature to new heights.

I also discovered that climate change was no far-away threat. Even during my few years of hiking, I saw lush forests and meadows fall dry and barren. My senior hiking companions explained that these changes were merely the tip of the melting iceberg. This realization left me with the pressing need to take action to prevent further devastation.

My community of senior hikers taught me valuable lessons and granted me a new attitude towards people, history, and nature. I look forward to sharing what I learned with my new community at UW and making friends by exploring Washington’s natural beauty together. I might even come across a bright-eyed young hiker and tell them:

“Are you hiking solo? Nice job, young man!”.

topic: Virtually all of Stanford's undergraduates live on campus. Write a note to your future roommate that reveals something about you or that will help your roommate—and us—get to know you better.
content:
Hey Roomie,

What’s got you most hyped for Stanford? Got any favorite hobbies? What do you geek out on?

Sorry if my questions seem overwhelming; I’m thrilled to learn about people I meet! 

Personally, I’m most excited about new experiences, whether that’s exploring the campus together, popping into Tech Talks at the Gates Building, or cheering for our Big Game win. As an amateur photographer, I’ll volunteer for a 4-year term as our dorm’s historian, capturing every laugh, adventure, and even tears we have together with my trusty Polaroid. 

On Fridays, you might find me playing the piano, singing to Coldplay’s A Sky Full of Stars and sometimes failing to hit the “I don’t care~” line. Ironically, I do care about many things, including beating my 7x7x7 Rubik’s Cube’s personal best (currently still at a humble two hours). 

When I’m not singing, I’ll probably be at the Coupa Cafe battling another boss-level problem set. Between caramel-latte refills, I’d be buried in VS Code, trying to comprehend why an algorithm that looks O(n²) is actually O(nlogn). (Seriously, how is the FFT subquadratic?) And if you ever need someone to debate random things, from the best coding language to which cafeteria has the best cookies, you can always count on me. 

So that’s a little bit about me. What about you? Can’t wait to hear your stories!

PS: Our pantry might be filled with my favorite Taiwanese snacks. If you’re ever hungry after a long day, knock yourself out!

Joseph

topic: Emory University has a strong commitment to building community. Tell us about a community you have been part of where your participation helped to change or shape the community for the better.
content:
My community has excellent healthcare facilities and insurance, yet this abundance sometimes results in patients overusing resources and nurses facing burnout. As soon as I reached the minimum age for hospital volunteering, I joined the Ton-Yen Hospital team. Although I lacked professional knowledge, offering empathy required no formal training. I still remember an elderly woman trembling after hemodialysis as her husband searched helplessly for support. In my broken Taiwanese Hokkien, I offered a warm pink blanket. Her faint smile, in return, filled the ER with a warmth that words couldn’t express. Moments like this reminded other volunteers and staff that compassion could ease workloads and restore humanity to a fast-paced medical system. 

With the knowledge I gain at Emory, I hope to extend this compassion beyond hospital walls by joining student organizations like the Atlanta Pediatric Cancer Organization, supporting families in Atlanta with the same empathy that first inspired me.

topic: Tell us why you decided to apply to the University of Wisconsin-Madison. In addition, please include why you are interested in studying the major(s) you have selected. If you selected undecided please describe your areas of possible academic interest.
content:
“Ah! So this is the root of the problem!” I exclaimed to Mom with a disassembled motherboard, hard drive, and graphic card lying around me.

Just like that, my first encounter with engineering came when I was in eighth grade with my attempt to fix my mom’s laptop. Throughout that week, my screen was constantly filled with YouTube videos that introduced complex hardware functions inside computers. Day and night, I constantly disassembled and reassembled the device, digging into the root cause. In fact, it didn’t take me long to figure out that the device malfunction was due to a dead hard disk drive (HDD). However, I was not satisfied with simply replacing the old HDD, especially after learning the features of a solid-state drive (SSD)—a more robust and effective type of storage component—from one of the online tutorials. To carry out my intended upgrade, I investigated if an SSD could be used with an HDD adapter and eventually bought an SSD-to-HDD converter, leading to a “life-saving” moment for my mom’s laptop while igniting my passion for hands-on problem-solving as an engineer.

Yet, growing up in the high-tech center of Taiwan, I happened never to get enough of innovative technology and development. With most of my relatives being engineers in the semiconductor industry, my interest in the field of advanced nanotechnology that we use in indispensable digital devices was further fostered when work was once again brought to the dinner table or another family session. Meanwhile, my curiosity about the applications of electrical equipment motivated me to take Advanced Placement courses in physics and computing to better understand the field of electrical engineering. Moreover, to get a better view of the real-world applications of integrated circuits, I took up the opportunity to intern at a spectral chip company. During my time there, I dealt with sophisticated electronics, such as the Pleroma Mini Spectrometer and HG-1 Mercury Argon Calibration Source, to identify poor-performing spectral chips by looking at whether their signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) level reaches the required number through running tests. Overall, this experience not only exposed me to the intricacies of handling chips, integrating them into electronic devices, and running simulations but also enlightened me on the vital role of computational tools in modern chip testing and design, furthering my understanding of the application of high-end chips and my passion for related fields.

At UW-Madison, with my aspiration to make advancements in sustainable chip design solutions for higher data transmission efficiency, I hope to drive into the studies of electrical engineering through major core courses, as well as prepare myself for a future career in the semiconductor industry with a solid foundation through advanced electives, including “ECE 445 Semiconductor Physics and Devices” and “ECE 549 Integrated Circuit Fabrication Laboratory.” In addition, to further facilitate my goal while putting the knowledge I will learn into practice, I wish to work under Professor Azadeh Davoodi to research the topic of explainable machine learning for better VLSI chip design. I am also interested in Assistant Professor Chirag Gupta’s research on optoelectronic devices due to my previous internship experience at the spectral chip company. I hope to gather more insight into how these optoelectronic devices could serve as the backbone of clean and green solutions and, from there, develop a theme for my “ECE 491 Senior Design Project” after further exploration of various topics related to sustainable design while adapting to the needs of solutions for innovative applications such as AI.

Beyond the academic setting, UW-Madison provides an extensive array of extracurricular student organizations that would enable me to persist in following the interests that shape my identity and perspectives. Through joining The Badger Herald as a newspaper editor for sports or science and involving myself in the Badgers Men's Swimming & Diving team, I am excited to continue my passion from high school to express myself while striding forward for the better.

topic: The Stanford community is deeply curious and driven to learn in and out of the classroom. Reflect on an idea or experience that makes you genuinely excited about learning.
content:
I love cracking life’s hidden codes. Whether it’s hunting for the optimal badminton footwork or analyzing how street lights time their cycles, I found joy in uncovering the formulas that govern the world around me.  

Human language, though, was a wilder puzzle I encountered. Could machines truly understand meaning? Is there even a formula? Hungry for answers, I delved into research studies and YouTube videos. There, I discovered how words like tokenizer and embedding matrix weren’t simply buzzwords. Each played a crucial role in teaching models to separate a 19th-century king from a chess king.

Now, at Academia Sinica, I apply my new insights into building translation models for endangered languages. Specifically, I design custom tokenizers that enable translation models to split and process Austronesian languages more easily. Because of the languages’ endangered nature and scarce data, I also turned to specialized algorithms like Direct Preference Optimization, a form of reinforcement learning that lets machines discover their own ways of structuring translation through trial-and-error. To further improve precision, I designed frameworks that combined dictionary searches and grammar-rule prompts. 

Watching my model outperform past benchmarks, I couldn’t contain my excitement. It never fails to amaze me how languages, nuanced and delicately chaotic, can be translated and deciphered by structured machines. The formulas that I once chased had become a way for me to contribute towards preservation, keeping cultures alive! At Stanford, I’ll continue this pursuit, creating systems that safeguard endangered languages while advancing the technology that makes it happen.

topic: What about being a student at BU most excites you? How do you hope to contribute to our campus community?
content:
As a high schooler, through being educated about the policymaking process, worrying about how to handle adulthood, how to pay taxes, and often feeling overwhelmed and not being able to reach out for help, I’ve noticed the issue of youth not being valued as much as they should be. I participated in the South Bay Youth Changemakers (SBYC) program to address the lack of youth empowerment. Through the program, I gained experience in improving youth power and knowledge about adulthood, politics, and access to mental health support, and I believe I will have the opportunity to dive further into this topic at Boston University. 

Many student-run organizations focus on social justice, such as the Social Impact and Nonprofit Club (SINC), which emphasizes the importance of students' social value in society. A program I would like to participate in is the Initiative on Cities at BU. Through this program, I wish to be more educated about our community. I am passionate about engaging in urban policymaking and proposing ideas that could positively impact society. Additionally, I want to gain leadership experience through this program and prepare myself for future job opportunities, for example, if I become a researcher in the STEM field, I want to be able to lead a collaborative group that can climb to success and benefit the community, and I believe this program at BU will prepare me for this. Finally, I aspire to take courses like WED YJ 201: Intro to Youth Justice & Delinquency and WED YJ 360: Positive Youth Development at BU to be further educated about how I can impact campus community and build the fundamentals for being a part of youth advocacy. 

Through these various programs at BU, I will be able to navigate this issue and have a positive influence on youth justice.

topic: Tell a story from your life, describing an experience that either demonstrates your character or helped to shape it.
content:
Switching schools is hard, and I stumbled at first. My transition to an international high school perplexed me since I had studied in a traditional Chinese-based education system since elementary school. When I had the chance to join the new school’s orchestra, I saw it as an opportunity to integrate myself. I could use my skilled ears to be someone.

I joined the orchestra as a violist, despite only knowing the violin. At the onset, I was overly aware of my limited skill in playing the viola. Like a salmon that had lost its way, I often bowed the wrong direction and felt isolated. Consequently, the conductor told me that he thought I lacked dedication. Trapped in a maze of insecurity, my fingers would tremble as I played. For half a year, I played in self-imposed isolation and with hesitation. 

As the semester went on, the burden of tasks grew heavier, and the conductor doubled the amount of pieces to be practiced. Sensing something was amiss, my viola teacher told me to stay after a typical Tuesday afternoon practice. Although I knew he just wanted to talk, I started to stutter. Afraid of getting punished, tears streamed down my face.

My viola teacher, Mr. Yang, spoke with an unwavering tone. He calmed me and said I needed to shed the self-imposed pressure weighing me down. Just go for it, don't overthink. Though tears continued to flow, I felt a sense of relief.

Breaking free from the shadow of shyness that I had cast over myself, I listened intently when the first chair violist advised me to loosen my wrist. Despite the ongoing challenge and isolation that surrounded me – my peers practicing the conductor's assigned pieces, leaving little room for connection – I persevered. I spent hours studying YouTube videos, refining the speed and precision of my fingerings. I practiced my bowings, tirelessly constructing the flexibility of my wrist with each repetition. In moments of respite, I dived into the musical sheets, swimming in every note and committing to memory each conductor’s notation on those sheet music. As a result, I started to gain the courage and strength to mute the discouraging voices inside my head, and my confidence began to return. 

The date was January 17, 2023, my birthday. Filled with determination to conquer my shyness, I tightened my bow and tuned my A string just before I began the chair test. Shivers ran down my spine, sweat glistened on my forehead, and uneasiness gnawed at my fingertips. In the end, despite a few inaccurate notes and missed bowings, I earned the position of head chair of the viola section.

Through the challenging journey of overcoming my shyness and embracing my true self, I've come to recognize the transformative power of continuous self-reflection. It's an ongoing process, one that requires constant effort and openness to learning. I've learned to embrace my shortcomings, understanding that they are not just hurdles but essential stepping stones for personal growth. Acceptance and bravery have become my dependable allies on the road to self-evolution, allowing me to face present and future challenges with a growing sense of resilience. These lessons continue to shape my path towards personal growth and success, serving as a crucible where my identity is ever-evolving. From a once-shy student to a leader guiding the viola section during the national orchestra competition, my journey continues. As I enter university, I hope to carry this mindset forward, recognizing that the process of self-discovery is ongoing, and each challenge is an opportunity for further development. It remains a powerful catalyst, propelling me forward academically and personally, just as it did in the realm of music.

topic: If I could travel anywhere, I would go to...*
content: Silicon Valley. Growing up in Hsinchu, the tech center of Taiwan, most of my family members are in the semiconductor industry. Their conversations are filled with technical terms like IC products, wafers, UV lithography, and, of course, Silicon Valley. Without Silicon Valley, Hsinchu would not have a semiconductor industry—but so does Taiwan’s chip industry supports the Valley. For a budding electrical engineer, SV is a mythical place filled with the world’s leading tech giants. I hope to immerse myself in this beating heart of the global tech industry.

topic: Because we know that diversity benefits the educational experience of all students, the University of Maryland values diversity in all of its many forms.  This includes (but is not limited to) racial, socio-economic, gender, geographical, and sexual orientation.  We are interested in hearing about your own individual life experiences.  In a few sentences, will you please describe how you have learned, grown, been inspired or developed skills through one or more components of diversity.
content: Living in a country where Chinese, Japanese, and Taiwanese cultures intersect, and transitioning from a local school to an American school, with a wholly different learning environment and language, presented formidable challenges for me. Yet, amidst the challenges, I’ve learned to be a compromiser, finding the middle ground between two seemingly opposing styles, and positioning myself in my comfort zone. I’ve now immersed myself by setting iPhone language into English, reading my favorite badminton news in English, and mentally rehearsing my speaking while taking a shower. I have learned the process of adaptation from diverse cultures.

topic: The most interesting fact I ever learned from research was…
content: To persuade a normal individual to commit murder under social pressure takes only about 72 minutes. "The Push" experiment undergoes a series of psychological manipulations and social pressures, ultimately challenging participants to push a person off a rooftop. It's fascinating because it reveals how swiftly social conformity can lead people to act against their moral values, exposing the malleability of the human mind as individuals adapt to societal norms.  However, in real life, when faced with pressures, it is essential to pause and reflect on our actions, asking ourselves, "What am I doing?"

topic: My favorite thing about last Thursday was…
content: playing badminton for two hours at the local university campus. The most exciting part of playing in a public badminton pool is to compete with diverse players. Each Thursday, I come across different opponents on the court, each with their distinct playing habits. This is when I can explore multiple tactics. When a new tactic comes into my mind, the desire to put this strategy into practice immediately excites me as if I try to anticipate what’s inside the Christmas gift box. New discovery and consistent improvement are what truly motivates me to play badminton regularly.

topic: My favorite thing about last Thursday was...*
content: Being able to gather with all my family members for our annual barbeque. Last Thursday was the Moon Festival in Taiwan, one of the biggest holidays. There is nothing like being together with my 24 family members on this day, chatting while having a grand BBQ that we only have once a year. This year’s Moon Festival was especially special for me as it is my last year to celebrate with all my family members since I’ll be studying abroad next year. I am especially thankful to have had such a supportive and connected family to accompany me through K-12, and I will definitely miss our annual BBQ next year.

topic: In addition to my major, my academic interests include…
content: My passion for Robotics & Autonomous Systems started with my engagement in VEX Robotics in my sophomore year. I delved into robot construction and autonomous programming, particularly in concepts like PID control, motion profiles, and odometry. VEX Robotics taught me the vital role these techniques play in achieving precision and smooth robot movement. Looking ahead to college, I hope to pursue a minor in this field to deepen my knowledge and skills. My goal is to then return to my high school and share these skills with those Robotics members, serving as a mentor to guide them towards greater achievements in this exciting field.

topic: We are looking for peacemakers, changemakers, global citizens, boundary breakers, creatives and innovators – Choose one quote from the following and let us know why it inspires you; or share a short quote and person not on our list who inspires you, and include why.
content:
“We’re used to people telling us there are no solutions, and then creating our own. So we did what we do best. We reached out to each other, and to our allies, and we mobilized across communities to make change, to benefit and include everyone in society.” Judith Heuman, 2022 NYU Commencement Address

My half-awake eyes widened when I saw the Facebook post. The spike in demand for online learning during the pandemic had led to a shortage of computers at the local immigrant assistance center. I dragged my brother over, and we recognized our shadows on the screen: two first-generation Taiwanese, yearning for something so basic yet out of reach. We went to the local electronics store, only to find that all of their computers were already pre-ordered. However, this setback only made us more determined, and we decided that there was no better way than to build computers ourselves.

We reached out to our friends, and they reached out to their friends, asking for donations of computers beyond repair. Soon, my locker was bulging with cardboard boxes of old machines and spare parts. A teacher at school helped us find a mentor to guide us through the assembly. The path was littered with mistakes: my brother spent 10 minutes trying to jam a stick of RAM without removing its protective cover; our mentor facepalmed when he saw my “morbid” wiring. Yet, before long, we had built our first computer. And then another. And another. Eventually, we completely fulfilled the needs of not one, but two local immigrant centers. Recognizing language barriers, we also filmed a no-talk instructional video demonstrating basic functions and Windows 10 installation. Judith Heuman’s quote inspires me because it is true: when we work together as a community, we can make the impossible into reality.

topic: In addition to my major, my academic interests include...
content: World history because I’ve always been fascinated by the massive amounts of information we’ve uncovered about the past and how it has shaped the world today. Also, I’ve always wondered what would happen if a historical event turned out differently than it did. One of the most exciting parts of History is coming up with your own arguments about how a specific event affected/caused another event and supporting it with historical evidence. Being able to make connections between past events is like solving a crime, slowly discovering why and how they occurred. I truly enjoy the linking process and the heated debates on these historical events.

topic: Something you might not know about me is...*
content: I almost successfully hatched chickens with a handmade incubator I constructed with a cardboard box. My dad had brought home a carton of eggs that he was expecting to cook for dinner, but I, being the adventurous kid I was, decided I would hatch these eggs. I first found a big cardboard box and surrounded the sides with towels to act as insulators. Then, I went to the hardware store to buy a 20W incandescent bulb to act as the heat source. After a few modifications to the box, I assembled all the parts and waited for the eggs to hatch. Every day, I checked the humidity and temperature levels, but on the 21st day, a power outage ended it all.

topic: If I could travel anywhere, I would go to...
content: Switzerland. My hometown is a bustling technology center, where AI and IC design dominate people’s daily lives. Conversations often center on stocks, futures, and the latest technological breakthroughs. My father’s long work hours gradually fell into this loop, leaving little time for us to connect. I want to visit Switzerland with him, a country renowned for its breathtaking landscapes and natural beauty. I envision us immersing ourselves in the unspoiled atmosphere, free from the pressures and worries of our everyday lives. I hope to share a deep breath with my father in Switzerland, creating precious memories together.

topic: Something you might not know about me is...
content: the deep-seated passion that has been burning with me since I was 7 years old. Since then, badminton has been my best companion. Engaged in intensive training, I set my sights on representing my school on the badminton court and seizing every opportunity to attend a vigorous competition. But my aspirations reach far beyond our school's courts. Driven by an insatiable desire to push my limits, I have a grand vision: becoming the state champion of badminton in my college years. Badminton is not just a sport; it embodies my dream.

topic: Because we know that diversity benefits the educational experience of all students, the University of Maryland values diversity in all of its many forms.  This includes (but is not limited to) racial, socio-economic, gender, geographical, and sexual orientation. We are interested in hearing about your own individual life experiences.  In a few sentences, will you please describe how you have learned, grown, been inspired or developed skills through one or more components of diversity.
content: I grew up in a conservative family that was also cross-strait: my father’s side is from mainland China, and my mother’s side is from Taiwan.  This meant I attended both public Taiwanese schools and private American schools. So, my life has felt like a constant juggling between 3 different cultures, systems of thinking, and learning philosophies. However, it also made me fluent in both Chinese and English, which allowed me to volunteer and teach lower-income students English every summer. I found great joy in helping those in need and, at the same time, developed a new hobby.

topic: Describe the people, places, events, or circumstances that have shaped the individual you are today. How could you contribute to the diverse and inclusive community at Santa Clara University?
content:
Growing up in Taiwan, a country rich in cultural diversity—from indigenous traditions to influences from China, Japan, and Southeast Asia—has shaped my appreciation for diverse perspectives. Experiencing these distinct cultures firsthand taught me the importance of adaptability and respect for differences, shaping my worldview and approach to collaboration.

As the leader of my high school’s iGEM team, I had the honor of guiding our team to win the 2024 iGEM Grand Prize. Leading a multidisciplinary group with members focused on research, marketing, and public health initiatives required balancing diverse perspectives to create a cohesive and effective approach to dengue prevention. Coordinating efforts with local public health officials and international researchers gave me a profound understanding of the value of empathy, open communication, and inclusiveness in achieving shared goals. These experiences reinforced the importance of uniting individuals with different backgrounds and expertise to address complex challenges.

At Santa Clara, I hope to contribute to the university’s inclusive community by engaging in the Ciocca Center for Innovation and Entrepreneurship. I am excited to bring together students from various backgrounds to work on entrepreneurial projects that serve diverse communities. I believe that by fostering a space where everyone’s perspectives are valued, we can create innovative solutions that address the unique needs of different populations. My experiences navigating multicultural dynamics and leading a diverse team at iGEM will help me contribute positively to the collaborative and inclusive spirit at Santa Clara University.

topic: Many students pursue college for a specific degree, career opportunity or personal goal. Whichever it may be, learning will be critical to achieve your ultimate goal. As you think ahead to the process of learning during your college years, how will you define a successful college experience?
content:
I will measure my success at college by the number of people I teach to whistle. I love whistling; it’s like carrying around an invisible instrument wherever I go--no prerequisites or music theory required. I’d love to meet other whistlers at CMU, or more realistically, join an a cappella group, but more importantly, I aspire to make my passions and knowledge as fun and accessible to others as whistling is to non-musical me.

For example, in high school I started a magazine to make scientific debate and discoveries more comprehensible to non-STEM classmates, and I envision creating a similar organization at CMU. I also see myself recruiting friends to Global Business Brigades, benefiting disadvantaged communities while helping non-business classmates understand that finance should not be intimidating, but rather a valuable tool for both career progress and social justice. Conversely, I hope to learn the invisible instruments my classmates carry around with them. I plan to join Model United Nations to learn more about international politics from an American background while contributing my perspective as an international student. I would also participate in ABLE CMU; as someone with multiple family members with disabilities, I seek to learn from and connect with my peers whose instruments are less understood.

A successful college experience includes learning broadly in class as well as extracurriculars. Like many Tepper students, I plan on complementing my major with a technology-related minor, most likely Data Science. In core business classes, I am particularly excited about finance and accounting, but I also want to take psychology and behavioral economics to learn about the more human elements that influence the behavior of individuals and firms. Most importantly, I want to keep enjoying the process of learning and sharing with others so I can always “whistle while I work.”

topic: Consider your application as a whole. What do you personally want to emphasize about your application for the admission committee’s consideration? Highlight something that’s important to you or something you haven’t had a chance to share. Tell us, don’t show us (no websites please).
content:
My father told me that, when he bought our house, he could see nothing but farmland in every direction. As I grew up, our community was redeveloped into a technology district, and I enrolled in the newly built international school on a scholarship. Unlike my friends’ parents, who all had advanced degrees, my parents never graduated from high school or learned English.
