However, my school was incredibly supportive; peers volunteered to practice English with me, and teachers provided hours of extra help. At the same time, I became a strong autodidactic learner. I practiced my English skills by translating TED videos, self-studied for the AP exams in micro- and macroeconomics, and eventually brought home the silver medal in the International Economics Olympiad.

I came to realize that success is founded upon two equally important pillars: access to resources, and confidence. This perspective inspires me to do everything I can to pass them on. In MUN club, I mentored dozens of students one-on-one to improve their stage confidence, sharing my challenges with public speaking to mentees with similar struggles. In 9th grade, I founded Investing Lab to teach students with no experience in economics how good trades stem from a clear sight of the market and its interplay with the overarching economy. Additionally, when my brother and I saw on social media that COVID-19 and the rise in demand for online learning had led to a computer shortage at the local immigrant center, we gathered spare parts and broken computers from our community and found a mentor to help us build computers from scratch. 

I will always be aware that it was the generosity of others that helped me overcome my familial disadvantage. I am dedicated to ensuring that people’s backgrounds do not hinder their deserved opportunity to learn.

topic: The University of Rochester motto of Meliora - or "ever better" - deeply integrates critical core values into all that we do. These core values of equity, leadership, integrity, openness, respect and accountability define not only who we are, but also who we hope to become. Please use the space below to highlight a time, creative idea or research experience when you put into action one or more of these characteristics in order to make yourself, your community or the world ever better.
content:
After several years of growing concern over the sheer volume of security vulnerabilities reported in web frameworks' update patches and GitHub repositories, I decided to investigate the problem myself. At Pioneer, I focused my research on XSS attacks, one of the most common online security exploitations; they occur when hackers insert malicious code into a server in order to steal or manipulate users' data. I found that, to prevent these attacks, web engineers should stop relying on third party-hosted files - a common practice in the software industry- and should invest more in training beginner developers to implement basic XSS defense, which is easy in theory, but becomes more difficult as the size of applications increases.

In web engineering, "integrity" means that a file is the same when it arrives as when it was sent. This virtual "integrity" is integral to user trust in an application because it ensures the legitimacy of user data. What my research found was that integrity was also key to equity: security exploitations, minor or major alike, disproportionately harm under-resourced groups who lack access to security software and expert guidance on how to implement security procedures, such as enforcing HTTPS. The solution to XSS attacks is to invest in more server space and developer training: in other words, investing in integrity to increase equity. Protections to the Internet's transmission protocols, designed to prevent XSS attacks, can allow more people to browse the internet safely, without the risk of their data or machines becoming corrupted.

topic: At Santa Clara University, we push our students to be creative, be challenged, and be the solution. Think about an ethical dilemma that you care about that our society is currently facing. This can be something happening in your local community or more globally. How can an SCU education help you prepare for and address this challenge?
content:
At the South Bay Youth Changemakers program (SBYC), I was given the opportunity to be engaged in civic and local policies by hosting Zoom meetings to discuss how much the local South Bay students think their schools value their presence and opinions in administrative decision-making. Through SBYC, I learned how teenagers’ voices about civil decisions are not being valued enough. Therefore, I would like to discuss the importance of teenagers and student involvement in local governing at SCU.

At SCU, I plan to learn how to improve and promote the scale of inclusion and racial equality in our society by participating in programs like Diversity, Equity & Inclusion Professional Development to learn the ways to advance diversity, equity, and inclusion. I am eager to contribute to this program by learning and practicing the skills and knowledge I have learned from SBYC when there is a chance to participate politically. This experience would encourage students to speak up and raise public awareness regarding how students and young adults’ voices must be heard before decision-making. 

Everyone is an integral part of the community and is an essential piece of the puzzle in building an ideal environment. By participating in social advocacy initiatives, SCU will allow me to advocate student voices and encourage students to speak up for themselves in political decisions since they are part of the residents of the community

topic: The most interesting fact I ever learned from research was...*
content: Coulomb's Law. Coulomb’s Law appeared numerous times in both my physics and chemistry classes, playing a crucial role in illustrating the behaviors between charges. It’s fascinating to see how the equation can explain phenomena from electric fields to electrostatic force and atomic radius. Learning this law opened me to the world of using equations to explain natural phenomena and motivated me to set out on a learning adventure, including AP Physics and AP Chemistry this year.

topic: Think about an ethical dilemma in society that you care about, either locally or globally. How can an education at Santa Clara help you address this challenge?
content:
I am concerned about balancing profit with public health accessibility in the biotech industry. Companies often prioritize profits, leaving underprivileged communities without essential healthcare solutions. I believe businesses can operate both profitably and responsibly to ensure life-saving innovations reach everyone.

An education at Santa Clara University, with its focus on ethics and social responsibility, will help me address this challenge. Through the Leavey School of Business, courses like "Business Ethics and Social Responsibility" will teach me to develop strategies that combine financial sustainability with social impact. The Miller Center for Social Entrepreneurship will offer practical experience in designing ethical business models for public health issues.

With these tools, I aim to lead biotech initiatives that bridge innovation and accessibility. My goal is to create ventures that prioritize both profitability and positive societal impact, making health solutions accessible regardless of economic barriers. Santa Clara's education will prepare me to drive change, aligning public health with ethical business practices.

topic: Most students choose their intended major or area of study based on a passion or inspiration that’s developed over time – what passion or inspiration led you to choose this area of study?
content:
Ever since my father gifted me Rich Dad, Poor Dad for my 13th birthday, I have been inspired by economics. I signed up for a macroeconomics course the next summer and fell in love with how straightforward it was to put everyday phenomena into simple numbers and procedures, like having a second pair of eyes to see how societies work. I self-studied for the AP exams in micro- and macro- and beyond, diving into mixed-equilibrium and Bayesian games, and eventually bringing home the silver medal in the International Economics Olympiad.

In spite of my achievements, it stunned me how much more my father understood about economics than I did. Although he dropped out of high school, he went on to find success as an entrepreneur, starting with nothing but two siblings and a boatload of debt. Whenever we discussed economic news and market trends, he taught me lessons that I would never find in the textbook, from logical leaps in my views on the labor market to forecasting a depression months before the pandemic. These conversations influenced me to become more interested in business. In addition, the more I read theoretical works, the more convinced I became of the importance business holds in society. For example, Schumpeter’s theory of creative destruction is put into action by none other than businesses; by championing efficiency, the market allocates resources in a way that maximizes productivity and promotes constant innovation. 

	

As I explored business through online internships at Forage, becoming treasurer of a fundraising group for the Yu-An Foundation, and founding the Investing lab at my high school, I found that the practical application made me champion it over theoretical economics. I will not be satisfied merely studying the effects of innovation; I desire to make an impact myself.

topic: Briefly describe what prompted you to apply to Santa Clara University. Based on what you know about SCU and our Jesuit mission to educate citizens and leaders of competence, conscience, and compassion, how do you envision your life at SCU and beyond? (200 words)
content:
My parents never attended college. As a first-generation student, I've always sought advice and mentorship from whomever was willing to share it. Hours of free help from programmers on StackOverflow and GitHub helped me get past coding bottlenecks. Seeing how they helped inexperienced developers grow, at no cost but for the love of expanding the coding community, I found that sharing competence is compassion.

These values of sharing knowledge and collaborative learning became my values. I devoted an hour per day over grades 8-10's summer break answering others' questions on Stack Overflow. I became a teacher's assistant in my middle school, helping students who were falling behind. I've found great joy helping younger students succeed, not only academically, but also providing emotional support. I hope to continue to share competence at SCU, where conscience and compassion are core values. I would participate in Drahmann Center's no-cost tutoring, join the school of engineering's Mentor Collective and further share my experience in writing research papers by participating in the HUB Writing Center. Just as I've gained from and returned much to online coding communities, SCU provides many opportunities to enrich my own and others' college experience by embracing a competent and compassionate culture.

topic: Why are you interested in Rensselaer Polytechnic Institute?
content:
RPI is a relatively small college with a student-faculty ratio of 13-1, which is especially appealing to me as it gives me the chance to form closer connections with professors, and also means it is easier for professors to put more effort into individual students. At RPI, I am especially interested in exploring high-speed transceivers and look forward to working with Professor Mona Hella on her research on high-speed circuits for wireless/optical transceivers. Moreover, as someone who has always aspired to find sustainable solutions to address the global climate issue, I hope to draw inspiration from Professor Hella’s research in energy harvesting interface ICs, gaining more insight into sustainable energy and how to implement such solutions in real life from an electrical engineer's perspective. Meanwhile, I am intrigued by the services RPI provides, for example, the Center for Career and Professional Development and the Center for Global Communication + Design. Both are crucial resources for me, as I am eager to gain early work experience in college and often seek advice on my writing assignments.

Beyond the academic setting, RPI provides an extensive array of extracurricular student organizations that would enable me to persist in following the interests that shape my identity and perspectives. Through joining “The Polytechnic” as a newspaper editor and getting involved in Badminton team as well as the RPI Engineers' Swimming and Diving team, I am excited to continue my passions from high school while exploring new interests.

topic: At Santa Clara University, we value our diverse and inclusive community. Our campus learning environment is enriched by the lived experiences of people from different backgrounds. What people, places, events, or circumstances have shaped the individual you are today and how you could contribute to our community? (150- 300 words)
content:
A sense of accomplishment bubbled in me as Jaden showed me his A+ for the biology final with a sparkle in his eyes. Having always struggled with this biology project, I was delighted to see Jaden’s growth as he worked diligently throughout the year. 

Walking into the room with intricately designed lesson plans, I quickly adjusted my breath before beginning my class as a peer tutor. During the first of the three weekly lessons, I would give my students a small lecture about the materials, like the four types of macromolecules, to lay the groundwork. Then, I would help them with their homework and teach them new concepts we find in the homework as we proceed. Lastly, I tested them on the topics with games like Kahoot! and reviewed the questions they got wrong. When my students were confused about the difference between saturated and unsaturated fat, I drew the structures of both kinds of fat with complementary reading material to enhance their understanding. Seeing how students gradually improved their grades after my extensive and repeated explanation, I learned that I should always try to incorporate various teaching methods to aid in my students’ comprehension. I should also give them more time to understand because not everyone can get everything on the first try. 

As a teacher, I renewed my knowledge while enhancing my patience and teaching skills, including curriculum design, public speaking, and self-confidence. At Santa Clara University, whether volunteering as a tutor or mentor, I aim to create immersive study sessions and lead group activities that make learning accessible and engaging for all my peers.

topic: Please briefly elaborate on one of your extracurricular activities or work experiences.
content:
EMERGENCY NOTICE: Without a faculty advisor, HCAS Times will soon be shut down.

I had just become the editor-in-chief of the newspaper club, HCAS Times, when I got the email. But I was determined to revive the club.

Our previous advisor had unexpectedly left the school right before the start of the semester. That meant we had less than two weeks to find a new advisor. I took rapid action, phoning and sending emails to the club members and various school staff, asking for their help recommending and reaching out to a suitable teacher. Within a few days, we had a hit: I contacted a new English teacher who was more than happy to help.

After settling the advisor issue, another issue arose during the school year regarding our publishing medium. Previously, the school had always printed out hard copies for us to hand out to the student body and parents; however, this year, the school would no longer do so. To resolve the situation, I sat down with the school management team to request reopening the printing and drafted a proposal to our Parent-Teacher Association to support it, but unfortunately, both appeals were denied.

All the pressure was on me, but I knew that I could not let all our members' hard work go to waste. I immediately gathered my club members with computer science-related experience and, combined with my own expertise, developed a framework for our online platform. Within a month, we were publishing articles online. Over the next year, it was rewarding to see this close community of three members expand to more than twenty, cooperating with the school administration and student organizations to deliver the most relevant news to the HCAS community.

topic: Why are you interested in pursuing the [Division or Major] selected above?
content: I am drawn to business because it combines creativity with problem-solving. I want to develop biotech solutions and market them, connecting my entrepreneurial mindset with innovation. Santa Clara’s Leavey School of Business will equip me with the skills to lead startups and tackle challenges with a socially responsible approach.

topic: Describe your personal and/or career goals after graduating from UIUC and how your selected first-choice major will help you achieve them.
content: With my career goals centered around making advancements in the semiconductor industry that will benefit the transmission speed of data in digital devices, I aspire to work for a company that focuses on sustainable solutions, where I can play a role in designing and implementing such innovative projects. Through SED and its unique Secondary Field Option in Environmental Quality, I wish to integrate my passion for technology with my goal of tackling climate issues and sustaining the human population. Courses like CEE 330-Environmental Engineering could equip me with environmental aspects to consider when carrying out unit operations. Besides building a solid foundation for my intended career through technical core and electives, such as ECE 110-Introduction to Electronics and SE 320-Control Systems, by joining practical laboratory experiences in the Holonyak Micro & Nanotechnology Lab, research projects, and internship opportunities, I hope to get hands-on while getting myself involved in the industry.

topic: How will opportunities at Purdue support your interests, both in and out of the classroom?
content:
Since freshman year, I have been invested in conducting experiments and performing data analysis with a sense of accomplishment. In sophomore year, I joined a research program that aimed to achieve biosensing, which is beneficial for developing mechanisms for early diagnosis and treatments. Finding that the diffusion model my advisor and I invented has been proven helpful in biosensing and filtering targeted molecules, I have officially set my final goal of studying biology to contribute to society by advancing medical resources and technologies at Purdue University. 

By applying for an internship position at Apple Medical Corp, I believe I can gain insights into the work of biologists and develop more field experiences by shadowing professionals. I also plan to improve my public speaking and logical thinking skills during my internship at Apple to deliver my ideas better. Furthermore, I am thrilled to participate in the cell and molecular biology program to continue my path in biosensing research on lipids, a prominent topic in molecular biology.  In terms of extracurriculars, I aim to join sports clubs dedicated to archery and basketball. With my previous basketball experience, I will be able to provide advice on tactical decisions and cultivate a supportive environment for the team to thrive. 

Purdue will allow me to pursue my goal of studying biology and refine my research acumen while exploring various extracurricular interests and engaging in a diverse community, and I look forward to studying at Purdue.

topic: Briefly discuss your reasons for pursuing the major you have selected.
content:
“The world is getting warmer? Great!”

When my five-year-old self first learned about climate change, it did not seem like a threat. This changed once I learned more about its catastrophic effects. Over time, I started experiencing some of these firsthand. I saw the lush forests and meadows on my mountain hikes fall dry and barren. I even felt the effects of global warming as summer heat waves grew more frequent and 40°C days became the norm.

My concerns about climate change found a partner in my fascination with nature. I go hiking most weeks out of the year. While I enjoy the physical challenge of a good climb, my primary motivation is to experience natural beauty. Tectonic movements and uplift have turned the sea floor into mountain crests, creating a dramatic multiplicity of rocks and landforms. I love exploring these formations to no end.

My love for the Earth and my concerns about our treatment of her have coalesced in a strong interest in environmental and geological sciences. This interest deepened during the recent Geography Olympiad. During the finals, I trekked through various trails, local residences, and tourist spots in Xindian and examined how coal mining culture and fluvial erosion influenced local land use patterns. Afterwards, I came up with several evidence-based plans for flood prevention and devised sustainable methods to preserve cultural heritage sites through tourism. This experience cemented my desire to study climate change and environmental preservation. Purdue’s Environmental Geosciences major would completely fulfill that desire.

topic: Please explain your interest in your second-choice major or your overall academic or career goals.
content: As I took notes on how chemistry knowledge can be applied to biology, some examples include environmental biology, food biology, nutrition, and cell membrane transport mechanisms. By taking Introduction to Biochemistry at De Anza College under the dual enrollment program, I have discovered my interest in biochemistry through lectures and experiments in class, especially in the cell structure and lipid bilayer portion. At UIUC, the Herbert E. Carter Lecture course appeals to me because one of its specialties is lipids in metabolism related to influencing molecular medicine and nutritional measures, and it is an opportunity for me to pursue my interest in biochemistry. Another program is Biochemistry Undergraduate Research, as I aspire to collaborate with researchers and publish papers related to our research project on biochemistry. Motivated, I am eager to continue leading an experimental project and become an independent researcher

topic: Explain, in detail, an experience you've had in the past 3 to 4 years related to your first-choice major. This can be an experience from an extracurricular activity, in a class you’ve taken, or through something else.
content: My first encounter with engineering dates back to eighth grade, with my attempt to fix my mom’s dead laptop. Throughout that week, my screen was constantly filled with YouTube videos that introduced complex hardware functions inside computers. Day and night, I disassembled and reassembled the device, digging into the root cause. In fact, it didn’t take me long to figure out that the malfunction of the device was due to a dead hard disk drive (HDD). Yet, I wasn’t satisfied with simply replacing the old HDD, especially after learning the features of a solid-state drive (SSD)—a more robust and effective type of storage component—from online tutorials. To carry out my intended upgrade, I investigated if an SSD could be used with an HDD adapter and eventually bought an SSD-to-HDD converter, leading to a “life-saving” moment for my mom’s laptop while fueling my passion for hands-on problem-solving as an engineer.

topic: Briefly discuss your reasons for choosing your major and your interest in studying at this campus location (Indianapolis or West Lafayette).
content:
Biosensing through the lipid bilayer membrane can play an important role in early diagnosis and creating personalized treatments. In my AP Biology course, I explored the realms of biological studies and found my interest in learning about how each type of cell and its components function and how, although they are minuscule, they play an essential role in our bodies.  

At Purdue University, I aspire to participate in the University of Toledo’s Summer Undergraduate Research Program. This internship opportunity will allow me to gain insights into the hard and soft skills required to become a biologist, practice leadership and research skills as an undergraduate, and better immerse myself in biology studies. Under this program, I will focus on molecular biology and genetics, mainly molecular biology, because it is what I have researched in high school and is one topic I enjoyed most throughout the AP Biology course. With previous experiences in this topic, I strive to bring my expertise, technical skills, and understanding of biosensing to the research group, which may be beneficial to making meaningful and pioneering advancements in the field of biology. 

Purdue University provides a wide variety of opportunities for me to pursue my interest in studying biology. I will bring a new perspective to biological science and commit to the university’s research programs and curriculum as I seek opportunities to refine my professional knowledge and research skills.

topic: Briefly discuss your reasons for pursuing the major you have selected
content: My concerns about privacy arose in grade six when I discovered my school could view my laptop's activity history. Not only did I circumvent this by dual-booting Ubuntu, but I also convinced the IT department to let students use their own laptops in school. Later, reflecting on my experience making web applications, I noticed that few coding documentations taught security practices. This propelled me to conduct research last summer with cybersecurity professor Dr. Hanan Hibshi on XSS defenses. Having strong opinions about making online data more private and secure, I hope to gain relevant knowledge in Purdue's Security Track.

topic: Explain, in detail, an experience you've had in the past 3 to 4 years related to your first-choice major. This can be an experience from an extracurricular activity, in a class you've taken, or through something else.
content:
Joining the Aspiring Scholars Directed Research Program gave me a chance to work with a PhD advisor specializing in lipid studies to achieve biosensing, as we aimed to address early diagnosis and detection of infected cells for diseases. Utilizing the diffusion model we made, we allowed the fluorescein solution to diffuse through a semi-permeable lipid-coated paper. Then, we used the fluorescein plate reader to identify the number of fluorescence molecules that passed through the lipid-coated paper; the lower, the better. Moreover, as a group leader, I mentored new members on lab techniques and safety guidelines while advocating the importance of biosensing.

Our findings showed that based on the molecules the biosensing model detects, biosensing can be applied to finding and devising new personalized medicine. Intrigued by biosensing’s profound application and biology’s intricacies, I was inspired to pursue biology at UIUC.

topic: Describe your dream nursing job and the characteristics needed to be a highly effective nurse. Provide an example of a life experience that showcases these characteristics.
content:
My dream job is to become a pediatric nurse practitioner and dedicate my career to supporting children as they reach their full potential. I aspire to take care of both patients and their families through effective communication and attention to mental wellbeing. I also plan on returning to my home country to advocate for nurses’ working conditions later on in my career. I feel strongly that nurses’ wellbeing impacts quality of care, and the average 1:13 nurse-to-patient ratio and absurdly low pay in Taiwan makes it hard for nurses to be good healthcare providers. 

I believe the characteristics needed to be an effective nurse are efficient communication, strong observation skills, resilience, and an awareness of personal wellness. My experience of learning to dance and eventually becoming an officer of my high school dance team is where I got to put these skills into use.

I joined the hip-hop dance team at my school when I was 15, with no prior experience in dance. I was immediately degraded with harsh criticism, judgmental stares, and a massive dose of self-doubt. Yet my love of movement and music outweighed the fact that I wasn’t a natural. I made immense efforts to catch up with my teammates, devoting all my free time to practicing fundamentals and meticulously going over practice videos to correct my dance moves to completely replicate my team’s. I learned to celebrate my own progress when it came to auditions as I was hardly selected to dance in performances, but I never stopped auditioning.

The next year rolled by, and after an entire summer of training, I was selected as an officer on the team. This was what I dreamt of a year ago as I was guaranteed a spot in competitions and performances, and mentoring new team members. I had a great time leading practices, sharing choreography cleaning tips, and planning performances with my fellow officers. Suddenly, things went sideways. Family issues impacted my mental state and subsequently, my performance in choreography. I began to feel overwhelmed by the pressure of being a role model when I could feel that I was not okay. I spoke with the other officers and we reached a consensus of me standing at the very back of the team, to just keep an eye on things. That gave me some time to work through my emotions as I began to remember why I started dancing in the first place and began to use it as a stress-relieving outlet.

I am grateful to have found my passion for dance. Not only did it strengthen my resilience with the ability to find progress in myself, enhance my communication skills through working with my teammates, and sharpen my eye in observation during choreography cleaning; but it also became a way for me to manage my stress. I’ve already found these skills useful in my daily life as a part-time childcare aide, and I believe they will be even more valuable as a nurse and advocate.

topic: Reflect on one way you have contributed to a community or communities. This might include a community service project, a group project for school, or a substantial activity to support your family (care of sick relatives, employment to financially help family, etc.) What did you learn about yourself and your community? How have you applied what you learned to other situations?
content:
I volunteered at the Swab4Charley event held by the National Marrow Donor Program (NMDP) because of Charley Bauer, who I got to correspond with when I was still in my home country, Taiwan. Her words of encouragement and reassurance played a big part in my committing to OSU, and I remember being enthusiastic to meet her, only to find out that she was diagnosed with Acute Myeloid Leukemia and needed a stem cell transplant. NMDP matches stem cell donors around the world with patients in need, so joining its donor registry and volunteering felt like the perfect way to give back to Charley and spread her kindness. 

The goal of the swab drive was to raise awareness about stem cell donations and convince those interested to join the NMDP registry. We aimed to persuade, however I was completely out of my comfort zone as my Taiwanese upbringing rarely encouraged speaking up and speaking out, instead teaching me to follow instructions and go with the flow. Yet all around me, the atmosphere created by NMDP volunteers was contagious. I started to observe how another volunteer interacted with people who came up to us and began mimicking her, one smile at a time. By the end of our 4-hour drive, I could confidently flag down passerby and explain how being in the donor registry could save lives. And I was also inviting everyone (even those who decided not to register) to write a card for Charley.

Speaking out was a new concept for me, but this experience made me aware of its impact. I learned that I could be persuasive by speaking logically and firmly and used this skill on several occasions to advocate for myself and my fellow international students having issues with housing and roommates. I also made it a goal to gain more knowledge on speaking for a cause and found inspiration on the social movements chapter of my sociology course. These experiences gave me a brand-new perspective on how effective communication really goes a long way, and how much impact one could make by just speaking out. 

From the NMDP community, I learned to let my passion show. The staff and volunteers brought with them positivity and a sense of urgency that showed they were passionate about saving lives with stem cells matches, especially when it helped one of their own. We were ecstatic when someone registers and gracious but visibly disappointed when they didn’t. The staffs’ example made me a lot less reserved in expressing my emotion throughout the swab drive and found that more people resonated with our cause when I spoke urgently about it than when I was trying to make them comfortable by being friendly and nonchalant. 

I feel very fortunate to have found Charley and NMDP. They not only led me to a great cause to fight for, but also gave me a great environment to build skills essential to advocating for myself, future patients, and nurses in my home country.

topic: Describe your personal and/or career goals after graduating from Illinois and how your selected first-choice major will help you achieve them.
content: I love our planet. I want to understand how it functions and geek out over all its facets – from my rock collection to the tectonic plates. However, I do not pursue knowledge for knowledge’s sake alone; I intend to build a career fighting the effects of climate change. Tackling such a complex problem will require expertise in a variety of intersecting fields, like urban planning, computer science, and environmental science. Geography and GIS’ interdisciplinary orientation thus provides a great starting point for my academic development. For example, I am inspired by Dr. Sivapalan’s fusion of sociology and hydrology, and I am excited to learn more about this approach. Moreover, the GIS program will teach me to model and analyze the effects of climate change through large scale geographic data. This will empower me to develop new methods of combatting these effects and help maintain our one and only home.

topic: Briefly discuss your reasons for pursuing the alternate major you have selected.
content:
Upon watching “The Mind, Explained,” I learned many people are suffering from mental illnesses without consciously knowing. Determined to delve deeper into mental health conditions, I took several psychology classes under the dual enrollment program at De Anza College. One case study we looked into was about a man with anxiety disorders and how his therapist slowly helped him heal and recover over a long time. From reading articles on psychological disorders to analyzing case studies, I have discovered my interest in researching the biological base of human behavior at Purdue University and learning about treatments to address their conditions. 

I am passionate about pursuing psychology at Purdue University because of the various opportunities it offers. I look forward to participating in the Counseling & Psychological Services, where I can consult and provide mental support to other undergraduates while facilitating my learning. I am also excited about the opportunity to immerse myself in the Cooperative (Co-Op) Education Program to shadow experienced workers and learn from real-world examples. To become a competent therapist, I aim to sharpen my communication skills in a clinical setting, being able to classify each mental health issue and know the appropriate treatments. 

My time at Purdue will be fruitful, allowing me to gain experience before becoming a successful therapist who can heal people and fulfill my dream of helping those in need, giving effective treatments to people to promote mental health and well-being for all individuals

topic: Northwestern is a place where people with diverse backgrounds from all over the world can study, live, and talk with one another. This range of experiences and viewpoints immeasurably enriches learning. How might your individual background contribute to this diversity of perspectives in Northwestern’s classrooms and around our campus?
content:
When I was born, our house was surrounded by farmland. As I grew up, our community was redeveloped into a sparkling technology district, and I enrolled in the newly built school on a scholarship. Unlike my friends’ parents, who all had advanced degrees, my parents never graduated from high school or learned English. Having access to the resources and community brought by “gentrification,” I became modernized.

Fascinated by the world springing up around me, I self-studied for AP economics and the International Economics Olympiad. Yet the more I learned, the more I realized how little I knew compared to my "uneducated" father, a small businessman who struggled for years before achieving stability manufacturing umbrellas. Whenever we discussed new theories I had learned from the textbook, he identified logical leaps and gaps in my understanding.

These experiences keep me grounded. I will never forget that great notions, from the redevelopment of an “underutilized” district to Schumpeter’s theory of creative destruction, are best understood by the people who live through them. While macroeconomic indicators and equations may give an accurate overview, true wisdom comes from everyday people, whether they are customers, entrepreneurs, or simply those whose village is going to be “modernized.”

topic: Northwestern fosters a distinctively interdisciplinary culture. We believe discovery and innovation thrive at the intersection of diverse ideas, perspectives, and academic interests. Within this setting, if you could dream up an undergraduate class, research project, or creative effort (a start-up, a design prototype, a performance, etc.), what would it be? Who might be some ideal classmates or collaborators? (200 words)
content:
I have been interested in hydroponic systems on Mars ever since I explored the notion in my school’s science magazine. Space exploration is not only a milestone in scientific progress but also a wise precaution for longterm human survival. However, among the top concerns is the Martian environment, which is devoid of water and nutrients. This inspires me to conduct research into food production on Mars.

As Mars holds vast amounts of ice, I would have a team of hydrologists evaluate the feasibility of water extraction methods on Mars. I would also have a team of biologists analyze soil samples to determine whether nutrients found in the Martian environment could fully supply the hydroponic system without relying on imports from Earth. Another challenge is the limited availability of energy on Mars, which necessitates the implementation of energy-efficient systems. I would collaborate with a design team to maximize crops’ efficient exposure to LED lighting, and Martian sunlight if an evaluation from a team of physicists demonstrated the potential for sunlight to substantially contribute to crops’ energy demand. This project would certainly take more than a semester, but I know that Northwestern would be the place to pursue it.

topic: How will opportunities at Purdue support your interests, both in and out of the classroom?
content:
I love our planet. I want to understand how it functions and geek out over all its facets – from my rock collection to the tectonic plates. However, I do not pursue knowledge for knowledge’s sake alone; I intend to build a career fighting the effects of climate change.

The Environmental Geosciences major offers a clear academic path to my goal. I see great potential in large scale data analysis and climate modeling, so I am very interested in advanced courses like ‘Global Change Modeling’ (EAPS 42000) and Data Analysis Techniques In Earth And Atmospheric Sciences (EAPS 50900). Moreover, my field research during the Geography Olympiad showed me the value of combining classroom theory with hands-on experience, so I want to enrich my learning by participating in studies on extreme weather or climate dynamics. I am particularly interested in assisting Dr. Huber with his work on climate modeling and simulating changes within the Earth system as that is exactly the kind of research I would like to do some day.

Purdue’s extracurricular offerings speak to me as well. As an avid outdoorsman, I am exceptionally drawn to the Purdue Outing Club. Having traversed all over Taiwan’s mountains, I would love to make new international friends while exploring America’s stunning landscapes. On a different track, I am also interested in the Purdue Equestrian Team. Horse-riding is a completely novel experience to me, but that is what college is all about. Whichever way I may go, I know Purdue has got me covered.

topic: Explain, in detail, an experience you've had in the past 3 to 4 years related to your first-choice major. This can be an experience from an extracurricular activity, in a class you've taken, or through something else.
content: Earlier this year, I competed in the national Geography Olympiad. My academic knowledge carried me through the questions and writing assignments of the local preliminaries and semi-final. However, the final round required real field research. My team studied the impact of the coal mining industry and fluvial erosion on the residents of Xindian. We explored various trails and visited local towns and tourist destinations. By examining land use patterns, we determined the former economic importance of the now-closed mines. Afterwards, I helped develop plans to establish and enhance flood protection in the area. I also devised several methods to preserve and stimulate cultural heritage sites through increased tourism to increase their economic potential. We received a bronze medal for our efforts, and I was nominated for the International Olympiad. Hopefully, I will get to represent my country on the global stage and continue my study of physical and human geography.

topic: The personal statement is your opportunity to tell us who you are and how your life experiences may contribute to your success in the nursing program and/or to the nursing profession. Things to consider when writing your story: - Make it personal. Provide us with enough information that we have a good sense of your life experiences and how those experiences have shaped your thoughts, perspectives, and will help you be successful in nursing school. - Keep the focus on you. - Describe the knowledge, the skills, and/or experiences that helped you persevere through an unexpected situation, helped you overcome a barrier, challenged you to choose a different pathway or go against social norms, encouraged you to advocate for change, or helped you to successfully accomplish a goal. - Make a connection to nursing and/or your nursing school success. Using the life experience(s) described, share how the experience(s) will affect your ability to be successful in nursing school or will drive your goals for your nursing practice. You can write about one life experience that best tells your story or multiple experiences. You are encouraged to proofread your work and allow time for revisions.
content:
I made a great friend this semester, J, a fellow buckeye who inspired me to dig deep into my motivation for helping others. 

I used to feel incredibly awkward around wheelchair users, often stuck between the desire to help and the consideration of whether they wanted it. I’ve often heard the indignant “I don’t need your help,” but still felt a sense of sympathy urging me to act. This was how I felt when I saw the NSPIRE weekly newsletter asking for volunteers to help a C4 quadriplegic student with their foley bag. 

During my initial meeting with J, he offered to help me keep track of my volunteer hours right after he said hi. I was so taken aback that I said I didn’t need them and immediately changed the subject. It made me feel sad that he thought I was there only to empty his bag, even worse when I confronted myself and realized I’d gone because I thought it’d help with my nursing application. I decided to go back the next week to get to know him a bit better, sharing stories of professors, exams, and roommates. I went back the next week, and the week after that, and by now I don’t need to “sign up” to help him anymore, I just text him whenever I’m around his building. 

Somewhere between all the meetings with J, something in me changed. The sympathy that had once motivated me morphed into excitement over a budding friendship. I started treating my trips to his building like making a short detour between classes to see a friend. Our conversations were rarely about his wheelchair, so it made me feel special when one day he confided in me about being uncomfortable with the automatic door button to his lab not working. I cracked a joke about making some changes to his chair and bulldozing the door down before assuring him that his labmates wouldn’t have minded. It was a small thing, but I was proud of myself for realizing why he decided to share. The old me would have been hyper focused on fixing the door ASAP and overlook the fact that what J cared more about was feeling uncomfortable. By his expression I could tell that my joke had worked–he’d felt awkward about asking for help opening the door all week and wanted to laugh it off. 

Befriending J taught me more about myself than I could ever achieve on my own. I now realize that my passion for helping people isn’t fueled by sympathy, it’s motivated by my desire to create a comfortable and supportive environment for those around me. My sense of fulfillment is the strongest when those I help feel safe enough to be vulnerable. This mindset will be my asset and infinite driving force in providing care for patients and their families mentally and physically.

topic: You have selected a second-choice major. Please explain your interest in that major or your overall academic or career goals.
content: Growing up in a family that couldn’t survive without coffee, every mug I picked up at home, without fail, would have stains at the bottom, which all of them refused to come off no matter how hard I scrubbed. I then began to ponder the chemistry behind it, recalling my chemistry teacher mentioning that acidic solutions can help remove stains by adding a chemical charge to stains' molecules, drawing stains away from the surface. Taking theory into practice, my attempt to fight years-old stains with vinegar turned out to be a huge success as well as an inspiration. Moving forward, from positive and negative charge to photoresist, I intend to further expand my knowledge in chemistry and passion for solving real-life problems to chemical engineering, hoping to contribute to the development and research of innovative technology in the semiconductor industry, such as mini-transistors and chiplet solutions, to meet future needs.

topic: How will opportunities at Purdue support your interests, both in and out of the classroom?
content: Because I've done research on preventing cross-site scripting attacks, I want to enroll in CS42600, "Computer Security," and work with Dr. Christina Garman on "Keyless CDN with Conclaves" to more effectively protect users' online security and privacy. I'm also interested in object-oriented and other database models studied in CS44800 "Relational Database Systems," based on past experience optimizing my API project by migrating from SQLite to PostgreSQL and utilizing PostgreSQL's JSON concatenation. Lastly, having mentored CS students throughout high school, I hope to be a peer mentor in the Bridge program.

topic: We want to be sure we’re considering your application in the context of your personal experiences: What aspects of your background, your identity, or your school, community, and/or household settings have most shaped how you see yourself engaging in Northwestern’s community, be it academically, extracurricularly, culturally, politically, socially, or otherwise?
content:
In the little bedroom I shared with my older brother, all the walls were covered with bookshelves. My parents were not “well-educated,” but they never lacked the spirit of education. They encouraged my aptitude for mathematics at an early age, enrolling me in AwesomeMath camps and successfully advocating for me to skip a grade in primary school. I inherited their spirit, self-studying English by translating TED videos and Economics by attending lectures on edX. I started proudly referring to myself as an “autodidact” when I came across the word on a vocab list. Every subject was intriguing. Browsing videos from the Youtuber Kurzgesagt, I discovered an unexpected passion for science, and when my Spanish teacher assigned an art project on Salvador Dali, I became fascinated by how various art materials can create harmony like instruments in music.

I love learning, whether through gaining new knowledge or sharing it. In high school, I founded Science Magazine to make science more accessible to non-STEM peers and Investing Lab to teach middle schoolers not to be afraid of investing. At Northwestern, I would apply to join North by Northwestern as a Science and Technology writer. I also hope to convince my friends to join the Investing Management Club and overcome the apprehensive attitudes that many have toward finance, and exchange my perspective on global politics at NWMUN Seattle. 

Through Quest Scholars Network, I would share my story with other first-generation students and mentor disadvantaged students in the Chicago area.

Socially, I will always be available with board games and tea with roommates and friends. I do hope that they like to play on the rug I‘ll bring, though – my desk will always be reserved for the books, notes, and stacks of papers that compose the ordered chaos of my academic life.

topic: Describe how you have taken advantage of a significant educational opportunity or worked to overcome an educational barrier you have faced.
content:
2:23 am on Friday. I gazed at the bright computer screen, displaying my first app: a rudimentary school tour. Every tap, every successful screen transition felt like a triumph. This first computer science project hooked me like a rush of caffeine, and I was eager to keep going. 

Feeling emboldened by this experience and confident in my ability to write my best code even in odd hours, I was excited to enroll in the summer program “Data Science Lab with R” at UC Berkeley and practice cutting-edge analysis methods on real-world datasets. There was just one hitch–living in Taiwan, I needed to wake up at 3:30 am each day to attend class. Some days, it took all of my willpower not to smash my alarm clock as the jarring buzz reverberated around my dark room, yet, with the intrigue of the challenging tasks my professor assigned, I would be revitalized.

Two weeks until the final project deadline, my head swirled with all the concepts. I chose to work on analyzing the relationships between sleep, health, and lifestyle. I pictured the 24-hour time block every day in my head, puzzling over where I could find time. 

“It might be possible to work while eating.”

However, issues started popping up. Weird graphs appeared on the screen, with data points scattered randomly. Columns of the data summary were properly organized but unexpectedly showed zero accuracy.

While there wasn’t a clear solution to my problem, I pieced together various suggestions from multiple help forums I researched online. While everyone had different issues, I could find the connecting points between them to apply to my own. 

As I put each piece of the puzzle together, the model aligned like constellations on the scatter plot with accurate estimations. In just a few weeks, I finally established a sense of control over what initially seemed an uninterpretable language. 

Hitting that submission button felt like taking a big step to another level. By succeeding in this early morning class, I am confident that I have gained the determination to face the academic rigors of college.

topic: Every person has a creative side, and it can be expressed in many ways: problem solving, original and innovative thinking, and artistically, to name a few. Describe how you express your creative side.
content:
On my 7th birthday, I discovered the first thing I truly hated: a spirograph set. 

I eagerly positioned the tip of my pencil in the gear, tracing around the jagged teeth, excited to replicate the intricate patterns advertised on the box. But as the lines danced across the sheet of paper, they seemed to follow their own chaotic will, refusing to obey the orders from my hands. When I lifted the tool, I was met with a jumbled mess of dismay. Frustrated, I traded the boxful of empty promises in favor of markers and paints—tools that bent to the will of my imagination, where every stroke was mine to command. 

However, I learned to appreciate the same chaos years later in front of the podium. The first thing I discovered about debate was that no matter how meticulously I arm myself with rebuttals, the opposing team will always find a way to tear down my carefully plotted line of defense. My first debate centered around the cliché topic of whether technology use should be permitted in classrooms. After my opponent presented a laundry list of potential harms, I was momentarily at a loss for words. Then, a thought crystallized in my head—I argued that the presence of risks does not justify an outright ban—much like it would be unreasonable to bad oranges simply because a child might choke on a seed. The analogy turned the tide of the round and showed me what real creativity entailed: creativity isn’t about mastering control but finding freedom within unpredictability and allowing the weeds of innovative thinking to sprout within the faults of preplanned structure.

This realization has molded not only my way of approaching debate but the process of tackling challenges in all aspects of my life. Whether it’s coming up with a new strategy to tackle a challenging sudoku puzzle or bridging incompatible groups together, I now see obstacles as a way to thrive. The motto of the spirograph now serves as the guiding light through my life: embrace the mess, work with the unexpected, and trust in the spontaneity that emerges.

topic: What have you done to make your school or your community a better place?
content:
The first time I met Dasha, the static of our language barrier felt almost insurmountable. Our conversation began through Google Translate; my fingers scrambled clumsily across the keyboard, stringing together basic phrases and greetings into the tiny box that bridged together our worlds.

Dasha is a Ukrainian refugee, but more importantly, she is the first student I ever tutored.  

Initially, our differences seemed daunting. Yet, we quickly discovered a shared love for horror movies and cheesy creepypastas after reading an article on the mummification process from ancient Egypt. Watching short films together became a common routine in our sessions, and we frequently traded urban legends from our own cultures, like the tales of Poludnitsa, a Slavic spirit, or ghost stories rooted in Taiwanese traditions. 

As we spent more time together, Dasha started to open up to me. She vented about the uncertainty of her future and the wounds left by war. I did my best to support her, letting her know that she was understood, even from afar. It was in these moments of vulnerability that I realized the most meaningful support wasn’t found in perfecting English grammar, but in simply being there to listen.

Dasha has shown me that compassion is a language transcending beyond cultural or linguistic boundaries. Inspired by her resilience, I joined various international service events, raising funds for children impacted by similar crises. Through Christmas cookie sales and volunteering at a makeshift nail salon, we raised a total of 7,000 NTD—small actions that, to me, held immense meaning. What I didn’t anticipate was how these efforts rippled through my community. My friends, seeing my dedication, began to join. Together, we reached more people than I could have alone by dedicating spare hours during our weekends to volunteering as tutors. By leading through action, I felt the power of turning compassion into tangible action and the impacts of our collective empathy. 

Moving forward in my medical career, I will carry the same philosophy that Dasha has taught me, treating not just the illness but the person behind it, creating change that inspires others to act.

topic: Think about an academic subject that inspires you. Describe how you have furthered this interest inside and/or outside of the classroom.
content:
I squinted at my screen, displaying a grayscale image of a chest scan, and observed the abnormal heart size. How could this machine detect something a trained doctor had missed? It was my first hands-on AI project, using computer vision to identify diseases from radiological images. Though the program my team had built was relatively simple, its performance transcended my expectations. From not knowing how AI processes information to utilizing machine learning and deep learning to perform such a feat, this was a significant milestone for me. This brief moment at AI Medical Science Camp cemented my interest in AI; I hoped to uncover the secrets behind these screens as I began my journey into artificial intelligence. 

Later that summer, I enrolled in the Inspirit AI program. I collaborated with peers on my first large-scale programming project—building an object detection model for automobiles. While expanding my repertoire of practical experience, this project also deepened my appreciation for the underlying mathematics of AI, as we trained neural networks to process data and make instant decisions in our model. From a simple neural network to becoming an expert model, our project improved from an accuracy of 72% to 95%. Observing how this project had the potential to mitigate catastrophic car accidents, I began to realize the limitless ways society could benefit from AI.

During the following academic year,  I took AP CSA to further solidify my programming skills through numerous projects, expanding on my previous summer programs. I also took the opportunity to present at my school’s World Science Day event about AI, from the big-picture implications across various industries to the technical minutia of neural networks.

Last summer, building on my experience in technology and tutoring, I interned for an educational tech firm, NaoNow. This foray into the burgeoning field of generative AI represented the culmination of my high school explorations. Looking forward to college, I am excited to see how my four years at the University of California will best prepare me for a career in this rapidly evolving field.

topic: What would you say is your greatest talent or skill? How have you developed and demonstrated that talent over time?
content:
The room fell silent upon the delivery of the final argument, stripped of its usual buzz of excitement as the teams shuffled past me, tailed by a concoction of discouragement and fatigue. As I watched my students retreat to their seats, I realized that the weeks of preparation for the World Scholar’s Cup had taken its toll, and morale was at its lowest. That evening, I pulled aside a few students and worked with them personally, moving beyond the isolating reliance on impersonal bubbles shaded on a rubric. As we refined their speeches together, I praised them for their strengths while tackling their weaknesses, reminding them that they shouldn’t be seen as failures but rather as opportunities for growth. By the end of the evening, their renewed energy was palpable, with some students requesting another debate the following week. At that moment I realized that I had taught them something much more valuable than crafting strong arguments—I showed them the importance of fostering healthy dialogue in an era torn by political differences, regardless of the outcome of a match. My role as president wasn’t just about guiding a team toward victory but mirroring the same passion that had first drawn me into debating. 

The same focus on encouragement has shaped my leadership across multiple teams. As president of the Science Club, I faced a similar challenge of members losing motivation as deadlines crept closer. Rather than letting my frustrations mount, I reminded everyone of our shared goal: publishing something we could all be proud of. Through consolidating a collective purpose and celebrating small wins, we not only met the deadline for publication but also produced a project that reflected our combined passion for science and innovation. 

Through interactions like these, I’ve grown to realize that my greatest talent as a leader is cultivating motivation and encouragement within a team. At the UC, I hope to continue developing this skill and foster communities where growth and innovation flourish—for both the individuals I lead and the broader goals we pursue together.

topic: Think about an academic subject that inspires you. Describe how you have furthered this interest inside and/or outside of the classroom.
content:
Sunshine is critical to a turtle's health, but no one wants to find a turtle with blood in its mouth after falling from the balcony during its daily sunbath. Panicked, I immediately looked for a vet, but the closest animal hospital with a herpetologist was a two-hour drive from home-I didn't have a driver's license, let alone a car. All I could do was say goodbye as my turtle expired.

I didn't have the skills to help then, but I swore to never be so helpless again.

The next year, I immediately signed up for AP Biology. From there, I got the opportunity to be a lab assistant in Tsinghua University Biomedical Laboratory the following year: here was the chance to gain practical experience with small animals, learning how to track a medicine's route through an animal via IVIS spectrum, quantifying cell volume with trypan blue dye and a formula. I learned so much basic anatomy, and also did special experiments like trying to activate the immune response in mouse bone marrow cells.

A month ago, despite having added a metal dog cage for safety, one turtle still managed to escape. However, this time, I was prepared. There was no blood coming from its mouth, but its shell had a clean crack running through its beautiful pattern. I checked whether the injury was mostly external, or if it had also affected the turtle's internal plastron. Looking at the shell, I knew applying hemostasis to the bone part - where the blood vessels are, rather than the layer of stratum corneum - would be key. Next would be a tough few weeks of giving my turtle the appropriate medicine and nutrients. But at least, my turtle had a chance.

Although there is a notch on my turtle's shell, it is now healthy and full of vitality. I've continued to work in the laboratory, and hope to move on to human anatomy, learning what it takes to be a biomedical technician. I'm grateful to have been able to save my turtle, and one day, maybe I'll be able to save humans too.

topic: Describe an example of your leadership experience in which you have positively influenced others, helped resolve disputes or contributed to group efforts over time.
content:
“This is your ticket to Seoul. The bus is scheduled to arrive at 15:15, and we will head to platform 30, just over there.”

I handed out tickets as we waited for the bus. A group of seven trusted me to lead the way after I spent hours researching and organizing the itinerary. Confident in our preparations, I was ready to explore the city with them. 

Having this unique opportunity to navigate through a foreign city for the first time, excitement buzzed in the air, but a weight like stone settled on my shoulders as I guided my friends through unfamiliar streets, bolstered by their encouragement. Checking my map every few minutes, I mentally prepared for the next step of the journey. While I had a clear sense of where we were going, I was still a little anxious.

Later that evening, we headed to Hongdae, just a few blocks away. However, the streets were jammed with people, and the chaotic flow quickly disrupted our plans. While deciding what to eat, three of my friends decided to go off on their own, leaving the rest of the group confused and uncomfortable. What began as a fun evening had snowballed into disorganization.

I spoke to my friends afterwards and learned that some felt the pace was too slow while others worried about getting lost. I realized the issue wasn’t just the crowd; it was a lack of coordination and communication. To prevent further issues, I suggested a system where everyone would inform the group if they had their own plans. This system helped us stay connected and organized while fostering shared responsibility and trust, allowing the trip to go smoothly. 

From this trip, I’ve come to acknowledge that facilitating a group’s success requires not only giving directions but also ensuring everyone is on the same page–just as I had to ensure we all stayed together in Seoul and headed back to the airport safely. This experience reinforced my leadership and adaptability, furthering my understanding of the importance of maintaining transparency within groups.

topic: What would you say is your greatest talent or skill? How have you developed and demonstrated that talent over time? (adapting, learning new cultures, etc)
content:
Born in New York, I have lived abroad most of my life: Shanghai for 8 years and Taiwan making up most of the rest. Navigating these extremes has molded my international perspective, and I easily adapt to culture shocks. Embracing my Taiwanese heritage, American nationality and Shanghainese influences has nurtured my skill of effortlessly connecting with people from various backgrounds. 

Shanghai’s cosmopolitan environment contrasts sharply with Taiwan’s more traditional ambiance, and the US is a mixture of lifestyles. A stark departure from Shanghai’s bustling commercial districts and fast-paced daily life, my rural home of Hsinchu, Taiwan, emphasizes family values and customs. During holidays, I participate in home clean-ups, cleaning staircases and picking up dead cockroaches. Although singled out for my Chinese accent, contrary to the customary Taiwanese one, I believe that my global mindset is an asset. Filled with stories from abroad, I am able to bridge the culture gap by sharing my experiences with my relatives and friends.

Living in the US for freshman year, I learned to appreciate its diversified culture and how the American educational system encourages students to voice their opinions openly, fostering critical thinking.  American emphasis on personal expression, self-reliance, and pursuit of one’s dreams is a direct contrast to the more Asian collectivist values of family, community, and tradition. Interacting with classmates from dissimilar backgrounds has enriched my perspective, and I love sharing my Asian heritage. I have become more attentive to small details in other people’s motions and habits of speaking, as they convey certain personality traits. My ability to adapt, appreciate, and respect these differences, honed while living in the US, has allowed me to forge strong bonds. 

My love for American culture drives me to return here for college. While country-hopping, I noticed that worldwide interconnectedness has become more apparent than ever. My experiences are not limited to any one country, but serve as a microcosm of the broader world, reflecting the broader interplay between tradition and technology. My unique background and the experiences I accumulated living in different countries equips me to contribute to disparate communities by fostering dialogue.

topic: What would you say is your greatest talent or skill? How have you developed and demonstrated that talent over time?
content:
"Don't you need sheet music?" asked my friend Nick as we practiced our duet. In that moment, I realized that even though I had never relied on sheet music, always playing from memory, that wasn't the case for most people.

Memory had always been my strength: memorizing 198 sample questions a night in preparation for an exam was normal for me, and I had assumed it was for everyone else too. As long as I applied a fair amount of concentration while learning something, it usually only took me one try. The Storming of the Bastille occurred on July 14, 1789 was firmly imprinted in my mind until well beyond the end of the semester after just one class about the French Revolution. Recall like this was handy, but what I've found even more valuable is my ability to recall my past mistakes, and so, learn from them.

Austin's and my science fair project using Gel Electrophoresis was due to be presented in just hours when we realized we were out of batteries. We were doomed: it was late at night in a remote area and we didn't have enough current to run through the two wires that powered our machine. We were exhausted and ready to give up when I remembered a project from elementary school. At the time, we had to light up a small bulb with a fixed number of batteries and wires. I remembered the feeling of disappointment from failing to come up with a solution; but just as vividly, I remembered my teacher explaining parallel vs series circuits right after. Series circuits created more voltage, but parallel circuits created more current. What we needed now was more current - I reconfigured our wires, and our gel electrophoresis machine turned on.

We went to sleep calm that night, knowing we would have something at least functional for the competition the next day. My great memory rescued and saved me and Austin, and even more so when our project won 'Most Valuable Biology Project' at the fair.

topic: Describe an example of your leadership experience in which you have positively influenced others, helped resolve disputes, or contributed to group efforts over time.
content:
Since age 10, scouting has always been my place to relax and have fun away from the pressures of school. I've spent most of my weekends and summers at scout activities; I've always been proud to be a scout.

So when our troop was almost disbanded last year for lack of recruits, I was struck by overwhelming sadness. I realized I had taken this community for granted, overlooking the invisible efforts of dedicated scout leaders. It was up to me to turn things around. I campaigned for and was elected senior patrol leader, taking on all the responsibilities from organizing meetings to directing admissions. Logistics were one thing, but I never imagined the hardest part would be training the new scouts and overcoming their resistance to our culture.

Our new recruits were characteristically rebellious and sarcastic middle schoolers. And no one was more so than Oswald.

Oswald had been forced to join by his mother, and at the welcoming ceremony, when someone asked him why he had joined scouting, he answered: "I don't care about scouting at all. I'll quit as soon as I can."

His lousy attitude persisted. As scout leader, I looked for ways to help him enjoy scouting. One-on-one talks. Candy bribes. Nothing worked.

One day, I noticed that Oswald's lock screen was a beautiful picture of a coral reef. A great idea began to emerge in my mind: that summer, our troop would do something we had never done before-a one-week scub diving campout. And I had an extra twist in mindt: a pokemon (Oswald's favorite game) adventure, with cards scattered in many places, even underwater, in a sort of treasure hunt.

I'd never seen Oswald smile so much as after that campout. He started to take part in activities, even walking at the front of the troop like he couldn't wait for what was next. I'd finally found a way to bring him in.

Being troop leader has forced me to continually be innovative with building our community. Though it is sometimes difficult, the sense of achievement from seeing my scouts engaged is priceless.

topic: Describe the most significant challenge you have faced and the steps you have taken to overcome this challenge. How has this challenge affected your academic achievement? (350 words)
content:
“Are ya ready, kids?”

“Aye aye, captain!” I hummed along with the joyful voices coming from the screen. My mother stood next to me, mouth agape, and then ran off. Soon, my family burst into the living room with smiles and tears. At age four, I was speaking for the first time. 

Despite my family’s worries, I liked being quiet. As I read and studied, my soul would drift freely in the ocean of knowledge. Noises were unwelcome incursions to the glimmering scenes in my imagination. Naively, I assumed everyone else felt the same way, and I developed a personal philosophy that I shouldn’t speak unless I needed to.

Unfortunately, often when I needed to, I still couldn’t. At an MUN conference, I watched silently as my partner gestured desperately for me to defend his argument. I reached for the placard, opened my mouth… and closed it again. When the resolution passed, without our amendment, I had spoken a grand total of zero times. The hardest part was facing my partner after the debate. There was no anger on his face – only defeated acceptance. Silent is just who I am. Or was it? I always classified speaking as an inherent ability, but with this defeat, it was time to change my mind. 

I trained hard for my next speaking challenge: the International Economics Olympiad. With my drama teacher’s help, I learned stomach breathing. It was not easy: I would involuntarily switch back every ten minutes or so and almost threw up multiple times during practice. And so I would train day after day, breathing in rhythm to tutorial videos.

The competition was a success. I spoke without faltering and brought back the silver medal. In the end, I gained so much more than the certificate in my hand. I learned that solid fundamentals are the root of all learning and that being natural is the best way to have confidence. I changed my abilities without sacrificing who I am. With hard work, even a kid who loves silence can be a great speaker.

topic: What would you say is your greatest talent or skill? How have you developed and demonstrated that talent over time?
content:
I watched Anastasiia’s cheeks grow flushed as she clumsily tried to pronounce newly-learned words; I fretted over the struggles I imagined she would face communicating with her new communities in Germany, where she had been displaced due to the Russo-Ukrainian war.

It puzzled me. Anastasiia has a charming Ukrainian accent when speaking English–series becomes sayleese, these becomes dees. Yet, despite having a decent command of English, she hesitated to speak both during our tutoring sessions and in Germany, where she needed to speak English more than ever.

I knew then that I had to exercise my skill of looking at problems through different angles to find a way to build Anastasiia’s confidence. 

Was it the words I had been teaching her? Epitome, phenomenon, obsolete–these were the words that I was told suited Anastasiia’s English abilities. As I thought from her perspective, I realized that she did not have the privilege to speak with carefully wrought sentences; she had to be responsive in real life. But when I started teaching easier words, Anastasiia already knew them all.

Was it the mechanics of pronouncing these words? I researched the best ways to break words into syllables and demonstrate tongue movements, but the detailed instructions seemed to help little.

Maybe, I realized, the problem was less technical than emotional–a problem of embarrassment, not skill. Hence, I started having her teach me Ukrainian words. There I was, struggling to pronounce a simple farewell, or [proshchavaj] in phonetic notation. She laughed, and I laughed too, in a shared understanding of the intricacies of language learning, of how our lived experiences shaped the phonemes we were accustomed to. 

Today, solving problems from different perspectives has become my greatest skill. From Anastasiia, who spoke bravely thereafter, I learned that what we perceive as problems are often entirely different from what we ought to solve. As a student in the University of California school system, I would strive to continue exercising this skill to devise solutions that would not have occurred to me otherwise, whether it be solving problems during classes or challenges faced by the school community.

topic: What have you done to make your school or your community a better place?
content:
I love animals. I have 6 turtles, two cats, and a dog. When I was younger, I always enjoyed watching all the exotic animals interacting in their natural habitats on National Geographic WILD. But I was heartbroken watching clips of sea turtles getting hurt by plastic straws and leopard cats getting hit by cars. I had to do something about it. So in 8th grade, I joined Boy Scouts and put my focus on ecological community services. However, even with these activities and the Boy Scouts, I felt I wasn't doing enough.

It was last summer that I heard there was a government volunteer environmental program recruiting new ecological volunteers. There was no way I could let this opportunity pass. Even though school was only getting busier, I still signed up for the training courses. That was just the first step: 30 hours of ecological training, including 10 hours outdoors in muddy wetlands while it was pouring. However, the image of the sea turtle suffering from plastics kept appearing in my head. I withstood the challenges and passed the training course.

Our duties were unrelenting: on holidays and weekends - including Lunar New Year - we had responsibilities of a minimum of 3 hours every day. During the summer, three hours felt even longer in the humid, scorching Taiwan heat. But no matter how tired or exhausted I felt, it was simple expressions of gratitude, like the ones from the Wild Bird Federations about our effort in rebuilding native birds' habitats, that kept me going. Knowing our efforts were recognized and valued, I would remember that all of this was for the animals. With that, my determination to persevere and make contributions even in the face of obstacles would be wholly renewed.

In fact, this year, our efforts have yielded a significant increase in the population of little terns migrating to the wetland for reproduction. Every improvement happens step-by-step, but added up over time, we can make a difference.

topic: Every person has a creative side, and it can be expressed in many ways: problem solving, original and innovative thinking, and artistically, to name a few. Describe how you express your creative side. (350 words)
content:
I stared somberly at the whiteboard full of mathematical equations. I then looked down at the four stubby lines on my notebook just to make sure. It’s the same. I sighed. All this work for the third equation of motion. 

I apply my creativity to my talent for efficiency. Take math, for example. Even though a formula gets the trip done, I often prefer to navigate myself through the streets and alleys, exploring every crook and nanny for a simpler path I’ve missed. Ever since middle school, I would mash equations together; whether it be for similar features, matching inputs or outputs, or even just for fun.
